PROFESSOR VIEW 01 / 38
BUSINESS ENGLISH — 32 Aulas

Milton Sayegh

Business English for Luxury Export — From dependence to autonomy in 32 lessons

São Paulo, SP B1+ Jewelry Export 60 min Online
PROGRESS0%
(inserir na stamps-row do header) -->
ALUNO
Milton Sayegh
IDADE
60 anos
CIDADE
São Paulo, SP
PROFISSÃO
Exportador de joias de luxo
NÍVEL
B1+ (Intermediate)
FOCO
Acadêmico com aplicação profissional
FREQUÊNCIA
1x/semana — 60 min
TOTAL
32 aulas — Online

Jornada de Transformação

Um executivo experiente de 60 anos que depende de intérprete para negociações em inglês, mesmo tendo operações nos EUA há mais de 30 anos. Sente-se limitado e frustrado por não conseguir se expressar com autonomia em reuniões e board meetings.
↓
Um empresário confiante que entra em negociações, board meetings e trade shows falando inglês com autonomia, clareza e fluência. Capaz de fechar deals, apresentar sua empresa e construir relações comerciais sem precisar de ninguém para traduzir.

"Em 32 aulas, Milton vai deixar de depender de intérprete e vai negociar, apresentar e liderar reuniões em inglês com a mesma autoridade que tem em português."

Forças

  • 30+ anos de experiência no setor — conhece o vocabulário técnico em contexto
  • Motivação clara e tangível — precisa do inglês para os negócios
  • Base gramátical existente (B1+) — não parte do zero
  • Maturidade e disciplina — perfil comprometido
  • Exposição constante ao inglês nos negócios — ambiente de imersão natural

Fraquezas

  • Dependência de intérprete criou hábito de não se arriscar
  • Fluência oral travada — sabe mais do que consegue produzir
  • Possível fossilização de erros gramáticais básicos
  • Ritmo lento — 60 anos exige paciência e repetição
  • Gap entre compreensão passiva e produção ativa

Currículo Completo — 32 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
01Diagnostic and Reactivation — Who Is Milton Sayegh in English?Present Simple/Continuous reactivation; business vocabulary probeSemi-structured interview: trade show first meetingRecord 2-min self-introduction audio
02The Language of Your Business — Products and OperationsPresent Simple for routines; jewelry/export vocabularyCompany profile presentationWrite company description paragraph
03Making Connections — Networking at Trade ShowsSmall talk; introductions; Present Continuous for current projectsTrade show networking role-playWatch Succession S1E1 with EN subtitles
04Negotiation Essentials — Setting TermsModal verbs (could/would/should) for proposalsPrice negotiation simulationListen to How I Built This episode
05Board Meetings — Expressing OpinionsPresent Perfect for experience; opinion expressionsBoard meeting discussion simulationWrite email summarizing meeting decisions
06Email Etiquette — Writing Clear Business EmailsFormal email structures; linking wordsEmail drafting and correctionWrite 3 business emails
07Numbers and Data — Presenting Financial InformationComparatives/superlatives; numbers and percentagesFinancial presentation practicePrepare quarterly report summary
08Review and Consolidation — Lessons 1-7All structures reviewed; error correction focusComprehensive role-play: full business dayRecord reflection on progress
09Contracts and Agreements — Legal VocabularyPast Simple for completed actions; legal termsContract review discussionRead sample contract clause
10Supply Chain — From Brazil to the U.S.Passive Voice; logistics vocabularySupply chain presentationMap your company's supply chain
11Problem Solving — When Things Go WrongConditionals (1st/2nd); complaint vocabularyCustomer complaint resolutionWrite complaint response email
12Cultural Intelligence — Brazilian vs American BusinessPhrasal verbs (business); cultural comparisonsCross-cultural scenario analysisWatch The Pursuit of Happyness
13Presentations — Structuring Your MessageDiscourse markers; presentation phrases5-minute product presentationPrepare presentation outline
14Phone and Video Calls — Remote CommunicationTelephoning expressions; clarification strategiesVideo call simulationPractice leaving voicemail
15Industry Trends — The Luxury MarketFuture forms (will/going to); trend vocabularyMarket trend discussionRead industry article
16Review and Consolidation — Lessons 9-15All structures reviewed; fluency assessmentBusiness lunch simulationRecord progress reflection
17Financial Discussions — Budgets and ForecastsThird conditional; financial terminologyBudget review meetingAnalyze financial report excerpt
18Building Partnerships — Long-term RelationshipsReported speech; relationship vocabularyPartnership proposal role-playWrite partnership proposal email
19Trade Shows — From Setup to Follow-upRelative clauses; event vocabularyFull trade show simulationCreate follow-up email template
20Crisis Communication — Managing Difficult SituationsAdvanced modals (should have/could have); crisis vocabularyCrisis management scenarioAnalyze a business crisis case
21Marketing — Positioning Your BrandGerunds and infinitives; marketing vocabularyBrand positioning presentationWrite product description
22Hiring and Teams — Managing PeopleConditional 2nd/3rd; HR vocabularyInterview simulation (interviewer role)Write job description
23Real Estate — Condo Board and PropertyMixed conditionals; property/legal vocabularyBoard meeting full simulationPrepare meeting agenda
24Review and Consolidation — Lessons 17-23All advanced structures; debate practiceFormal debate: industry topicRecord 3-min opinion speech
25Global Economy — International Trade ContextComplex sentences; economic vocabularyEconomic briefing presentationRead The Economist article
26Ethics in Business — Values and DecisionsSubjunctive; ethics vocabularyEthical dilemma discussionWrite position paper
27Innovation — Future of the Jewelry IndustryFuture Perfect/Continuous; technology vocabularyInnovation pitchResearch industry innovation
28Storytelling — Your Professional JourneyNarrative tenses review; storytelling structures7-minute professional storyWrite professional bio
29Formal Events — Speeches and ToastsFormal register; speech structuresGala speech preparationWrite and practice short speech
30Final Review — Comprehensive AssessmentAll structures; advanced productionFull business scenario assessmentComplete self-evaluation
31Confidence Building — Your New English IdentityFluency strategies; self-correction techniquesFree discussion: your English journeyRecord final reflection
32Celebration — From Dependence to AutonomyReview and celebrationMilton's graduation presentationShare progress with someone

Metodologia

Task-Based Learning

Cada aula gira em torno de uma tarefa real do universo de Milton: negociar, apresentar, liderar reunião. A língua é ferramenta, não fim.

Grammar Discovery

Milton descobre regras a partir de exemplos contextualizados. Nunca regra antes do uso. Indutivo sempre.

Spaced Repetition

Vocabulário retorna em warm-ups, diálogos e exercícios de aulas posteriores. Revisão orgânica e cumulativa.

Scaffolded Production

De guided a semi-free a free. Milton sempre tem apoio antes de voar sozinho.

Delayed Error Correction

Erros são anotados durante production e corrigidos depois, sem interromper a fluência.

Mapa de Personalidade

RITMO
Calmo e ponderado. Prefere pensar antes de falar. Respeitar o silêncio — não apressar.
MOTIVAÇÃO
Altíssima — depende de intérprete e quer autonomia. Motivação intrínseca e extrínseca.
ESTILO
Estruturado e analítico. Gosta de entender a lógica. Gramática explícita funciona bem.
TOLERÂNCIA AO ERRO
Moderada. Precisa de encorajamento constante. Perfeccionista — pode travar por medo de errar.
CONTEXTO IDEAL
Negócios reais: trade shows, board meetings, calls com distribuidores americanos.
TOM DO PROFESSOR
Respeitoso, paciente, profissional. Nunca infantilizar. Tratar como o executivo que ele é.
ALERTAS
60 anos = ritmo mais lento, repetição é bem-vinda, pausas naturais. Não comparar com alunos jovens.

Bem-vindo, Milton!

Este é o seu material de Pre-class. Complete os exercícios antes de cada aula para aproveitar ao máximo o tempo com seu professor.

"A gente se faz entender lá. Mas eu gostaria de me expressar um pouco melhor."

Frases de Emergência

I am sorry, I did not quite catch that.
Desculpe, não entendi bem.
Could you say that again, please?
Poderia repetir, por favor?
Let me think about that for a moment.
Deixe-me pensar um momento.
What I mean is...
O que eu quero dizer é...
Aula 01 — Pre-class

Diagnostic and Reactivation

Who Is Milton Sayegh in English? — Present Simple & Present Continuous
0%
▼

Vocabulary Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Estude cada palavra, ouça a pronúncia e leia o exemplo.

Export•Exportar / Exportação
To send goods to another country for sale
"My company exports luxury jewelry to the United States."
Luxury Goods•Artigos de luxo
Expensive, high-quality products
"We specialize in luxury goods for the international market."
Jewelry•Joias / Joalheria
Decorative items like rings, necklaces, bracelets
"The jewelry industry requires precision and trust."
Board Meeting•Reunião do conselho
A formal meeting of company directors
"I attend the board meeting at my condo in Miami every month."
Negotiation•Negociação
A discussion to reach a business agreement
"A good negotiation requires clarity and confidence."
Deal•Acordo / Negócio
A business agreement or transaction
"We closed a major deal with a new distributor last quarter."
Headquarters•Sede / Matriz
The main office of a company
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo, but we operate in the U.S."
Overseas•No exterior / Ultramar
In or to a foreign country across the sea
"I travel overseas at least four times a year."
Misunderstanding•Mal-entendido
A failure to understand something correctly
"I want to avoid any misunderstanding in important meetings."
Fluency•Fluência
The ability to speak a language smoothly
"My goal is to recover my fluency in English."

Vocabulary Stage 1.2: Matching

Associe cada palavra em inglês com a tradução correta em português.

Export
Luxury Goods
Jewelry
Board Meeting
Negotiation
Deal
Headquarters
Overseas
Misunderstanding
Fluency

Grammar Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Leia o texto abaixo. As estruturas gramáticais estão em negrito. Depois, responda as perguntas.

Milton Sayegh is a luxury jewelry exporter from São Paulo, Brazil. He runs a company that exports precious stones and gold pieces to the United States. His headquarters is in São Paulo, but he also has an office in Miami. Milton travels overseas several times a year for business.

Right now, Milton is working on improving his English. He is taking lessons because he wants to negotiate without an interpreter. His company is expanding operations in the American market, and he believes that clear communication is the key to avoiding misunderstandings in important meetings.
1. What does Milton's company export?
A Technology products
B Precious stones and gold pieces
C Coffee and agricultural goods
2. Why is Milton taking English lessons?
A He wants to move to the United States
B His company requires all employees to speak English
C He wants to negotiate without an interpreter
3. Which verb forms describe CURRENT/TEMPORARY actions in the text?
A "Runs", "Exports", "Travels"
B "Is working", "Is taking", "Is expanding"
C "Is", "Has", "Believes"

Grammar Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Revise a diferença entre Present Simple e Present Continuous.

Present Simple vs Present Continuous
PRESENT SIMPLE PRESENT CONTINUOUS
Facts and routines
Fatos e rotinas		Current/temporary actions
Ações atuais/temporárias
I export jewelry.
Eu exporto joias.		I am working on my English.
Estou trabalhando no meu inglês.
We specialize in luxury goods.
Nós nos especializamos em artigos de luxo.		We are expanding operations.
Estamos expandindo operações.
Milton travels overseas.
Milton viajá para o exterior.		Milton is recovering his fluency.
Milton está recuperando sua fluência.
Structure: Subject + Verb(s)Structure: Subject + am/is/are + Verb-ing

Practice Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Complete as frases com a forma correta do verbo. Use Present Simple ou Present Continuous.

"My company luxury jewelry to the United States."
"I on improving my English."
"We in luxury goods."
"Our company operations in the U.S."
"Milton overseas at least four times a year."
"A good requires clarity and confidence."
"I want to avoid any in important meetings."
"My goal is to recover my in English."

Order Stage 2: Self-Introduction Order

Organize as frases na ordem lógica para uma apresentação pessoal profissional.

?"We operate in the United States and Brazil."
?"Good morning. My name is Milton Sayegh."
?"It is a pleasure to meet you."
?"I run a luxury jewelry export company."
?"I am from São Paulo, Brazil."

Speaking Stage 3: Pronunciation

Ouça cada frase, grave sua voz e compare a pronúncia.

My name is Milton Sayegh. I am in the jewelry business.
I am responsible for the export operations of my company.
Our headquarters is in São Paulo, but we operate in the U.S.
I travel overseas several times a year.
Could you say that again, please?

Quiz Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação profissional.

You meet a potential business partner at a jewelry trade show. How do you introduce yourself?
A "Hey, I sell some stuff from Brazil."
B "Good morning. My name is Milton Sayegh. I run a luxury jewelry export company based in São Paulo."
C "I am Milton. I have jewelry."
Your American distributor speaks too fast during a call. What do you say?
A "I don't understand your English."
B Nothing — just pretend you understood.
C "Could you say that again, please? I want to make sure I understand correctly."
Someone asks: "How long have you been in business?" What is the best answer?
A "For over thirty years. We specialize in luxury goods for the international market."
B "I have 30 years of business."
C "Since many time ago."
You want to describe what your company does right now (temporary expansion). Which sentence is correct?
A "We expand operations in the U.S."
B "We are expanding operations in the U.S."
C "We expanded operations in the U.S."

Reflection Stage 5: Free Production

Grave sua resposta livremente. Não há certo ou errado — o objetivo é práticar.

Imagine you are at a jewelry trade show in New York. You meet an American distributor who asks: "Tell me about your company." Record your answer in English. Try to include: your name, your company, what you export, where your headquarters is, and how long you have been in business.

Survival Card — Lesson 1

1My name is Milton Sayegh. I am in the jewelry business.Meu nome é Milton Sayegh. Eu trabalho no ramo de joias.
2I am responsible for all export operations.Eu sou responsável por todas as operações de exportação.
3Our headquarters is in São Paulo, but we operate in the U.S.Nossa sede é em São Paulo, mas operamos nos EUA.
4I travel overseas several times a year.Eu viajo para o exterior várias vezes por ano.
5Could you say that again, please?Poderia repetir, por favor?

Checklist — Aula 1

  • Estudei as 10 palavras de vocabulário
  • Completei o matching
  • Li o texto de Grammar in Context
  • Revisei o Grammar Tip
  • Completei os fill-in-the-blanks
  • Organizei a self-introduction
  • Pratiquei a pronúncia (5 frases)
  • Respondi o quiz situacional
  • Gravei a produção livre
Aula 02 — Pre-class

The Language of Your Business

Products and Operations — Present Simple (Deep Dive)
0%
▼

Vocabulary Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Estude cada palavra, ouça a pronúncia e leia o exemplo.

Specialize•Especializar-se
To focus on a particular area of work
"We specialize in premium Brazilian gemstones and custom gold designs."
Retail•Varejo
Selling directly to consumers
"Some of our pieces are available in retail stores."
Wholesale•Atacado
Selling in large quantities to businesses
"We sell wholesale to retailers across the country."
Distributor•Distribuidor
A company that supplies goods to stores
"A reliable distributor is essential for international sales."
Supply Chain•Cadeia de suprimentos
The process from production to delivery
"Our supply chain covers sourcing to delivery."
Revenue•Receita / Faturamento
Income from business activities
"Our revenue has grown steadily over the past five years."
Market Share•Participação de mercado
Percentage of total sales in a market
"We currently hold a small but growing market share in the U.S."
Client•Cliente
A customer, especially of a professional service
"Our clients value quality and exclusivity."
Competitor•Concorrente
A rival company
"Our competitors do not offer the same level of craftsmanship."
Partnership•Parceria
A business relationship between companies
"I would like to discuss a possible partnership."

Vocabulary Stage 1.2: Matching

Associe cada palavra em inglês com a tradução correta em português.

Specialize
Retail
Wholesale
Distributor
Supply Chain
Revenue
Market Share
Client
Competitor
Partnership

Grammar Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Leia o texto abaixo. As estruturas gramáticais estão em negrito. Depois, responda as perguntas.

Milton Sayegh runs a luxury jewelry company in São Paulo. His company specializes in premium Brazilian gemstones and custom gold designs. They export their products to the United States through a network of distributors.

Milton does not sell directly to consumers. Instead, he works with wholesale partners and retail chains. His supply chain covers everything from sourcing to delivery. The company does not have many competitors in the premium segment.

Does Milton work with American clients? Yes, he does. He currently supplies three major distributors and wants to establish direct retail partnerships. His revenue comes from both wholesale and export operations, and his market share is growing steadily.
1. How does Milton sell his products?
A He sells directly to consumers in stores
B He works with wholesale partners and distributors
C He only sells online
2. Which sentence from the text uses the NEGATIVE form of Present Simple?
A "His company specializes in premium Brazilian gemstones."
B "Milton does not sell directly to consumers."
C "Does Milton work with American clients?"
3. What does Milton want to establish in the U.S.?
A A new headquarters
B A jewelry factory
C Direct retail partnerships

Grammar Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Revise o Present Simple em detalhes: afirmativa, negativa e interrogativa.

Present Simple — Deep Dive
FORM STRUCTURE EXAMPLE
Affirmative
Afirmativa		I/You/We/They + verb
He/She/It + verbs		Milton exports jewelry.
Milton exporta joias.
Negative
Negativa		I/You/We/They + do not + verb
He/She/It + does not + verb		He does not sell directly.
Ele não vende diretamente.
Interrogative
Interrogativa		Do + I/you/we/they + verb?
Does + he/she/it + verb?		Does Milton work with clients?
Milton trabalha com clientes?
3rd person -s
Regra do -s		export → exports
specialize → specializes		His company specializes in luxury goods.
A empresa dele se especializa em artigos de luxo.
Atenção: Na negativa e interrogativa com does, o verbo principal volta para a forma BASE (sem -s).
✗ "He does not sells" → ✓ "He does not sell"
✗ "Does he exports?" → ✓ "Does he export?"

Practice Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Complete as frases com a forma correta do Present Simple (afirmativa, negativa ou interrogativa).

"Our company in Brazilian gemstones."
"We with distributors in the United States."
"Milton directly to consumers."
"Our comes from wholesale and export operations."
" your company sell retail or wholesale?"
"The company retail partnerships yet."
"We manage the entire ourselves."
"Our include distributors across three states."

Order Stage 2: Company Profile Order

Organize as frases na ordem lógica para apresentar sua empresa a um parceiro.

?"We work with distributors in the United States."
?"I would like to discuss a possible partnership."
?"Our company specializes in premium Brazilian gemstones."
?"Our supply chain covers everything from sourcing to delivery."
?"We do not sell directly to consumers — we work through wholesale partners."

Speaking Stage 3: Pronunciation

Ouça cada frase, grave sua voz e compare a pronúncia.

We specialize in luxury Brazilian gemstones.
Our supply chain covers sourcing to delivery.
We do not sell directly to consumers — we work through distributors.
Does your company have experience with imported goods?
I would like to discuss a possible partnership.

Quiz Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação profissional.

A potential retail partner asks: "What does your company specialize in?" What do you say?
A "We have many things of Brazil."
B "We specialize in premium Brazilian gemstones and custom gold designs."
C "My company is specialized in jewels."
Someone asks if you sell directly to consumers. You do not. How do you answer?
A "No, we not sell to consumers."
B "No, we do not sell directly to consumers. We work through distributors."
C "We does not sell retail."
You want to ask a potential partner about their market share. Which question is correct?
A "How much market share does you have?"
B "What is your market share has?"
C "What is your current market share in the U.S.?"
Which sentence correctly uses the 3rd person -s rule?
A "He export jewelry to three countries."
B "His company specializes in luxury goods."
C "The distributor work with many brands."

Reflection Stage 5: Free Production

Grave sua resposta livremente. Não há certo ou errado — o objetivo é práticar.

Imagine you are meeting a potential retail partner from California. Describe your company in English. Try to include: what your company specializes in, how you sell your products (wholesale/distributors), your supply chain, your competitors, and why a partnership would be beneficial.

Survival Card — Lesson 2

1We specialize in luxury Brazilian gemstones.Nós nos especializamos em pedras preciosas brasileiras de luxo.
2Our supply chain covers sourcing to delivery.Nossa cadeia de suprimentos cobre desde a obtenção até a entrega.
3We do not sell directly to consumers — we work through distributors.Não vendemos diretamente — trabalhamos por meio de distribuidores.
4Does your company have experience with imported goods?Sua empresa tem experiência com produtos importados?
5I would like to discuss a possible partnership.Eu gostaria de discutir uma possível parceria.

Aula 03 — Pre-class

Making Connections

Networking at Trade Shows — Present Continuous
0%
▼

Vocabulary Warm-up: Lesson 2 Review

Antes de começar, revise 5 palavras da aula anterior.

Specialize•Especializar-se
To focus on a particular area of work
Distributor•Distribuidor
A company that supplies goods to stores
Supply Chain•Cadeia de suprimentos
The process from production to delivery
Revenue•Receita / Faturamento
Income from business activities
Partnership•Parceria
A business relationship between companies

Vocabulary Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Estude cada palavra, ouça a pronúncia e leia o exemplo.

Networking•Construção de contatos
Building professional relationships
"We are actively networking to find the right partners."
Trade Show•Feira de negócios
An exhibition for a specific industry
"I am attending a jewelry trade show this week."
Booth•Estande
A stand at a trade show
"Your booth looks amazing."
Prospect•Cliente em potencial
A potential client or partner
"Lisa is a strong prospect for a retail partnership."
Pitch•Apresentação rápida
A short presentation to sell an idea
"I like your pitch. Do you have a business card?"
Follow-up•Acompanhamento
Contact after a meeting to continue
"Could I schedule a follow-up call next week?"
Business Card•Cartão de visita
A small card with contact information
"Here is my business card. Could I schedule a follow-up?"
Schedule•Agendar / Agenda
To arrange a time for something
"Could I schedule a follow-up call next week?"
Industry•Setor / Indústria
A particular type of business
"The jewelry industry is very competitive."
Impression•Impressão
An opinion formed about someone
"Your collection made a great impression."

Vocabulary Stage 1.2: Matching

Associe cada palavra em inglês com a tradução correta em português.

Networking
Trade Show
Booth
Prospect
Pitch
Follow-up
Business Card
Schedule
Industry
Impression

Grammar Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Leia o texto abaixo. As estruturas gramáticais estão em negrito. Depois, responda as perguntas.

Milton is attending a jewelry trade show in Miami this week. He is showcasing his latest collection at his booth. Several prospects are visiting his stand today.

Right now, Milton is networking with Lisa Park, a retail buyer from California. She is looking for luxury jewelry suppliers for her stores. Milton is giving her his pitch about the company.

Is Milton making a good impression? Yes, he is. Lisa wants his business card and is asking to schedule a follow-up call. Milton is not looking for wholesale partners right now — he is focusing on direct retail partnerships in the industry.
1. What is Milton doing at the trade show?
A He is buying jewelry from other companies
B He is showcasing his latest collection and networking
C He is closing a deal with a wholesale partner
2. Which sentence uses the Present Continuous NEGATIVE form?
A "Milton is attending a jewelry trade show."
B "Milton is not looking for wholesale partners right now."
C "Is Milton making a good impression?"
3. What does Lisa want to do after the conversation?
A She wants to visit Milton in São Paulo
B She wants to schedule a follow-up call
C She wants to become a distributor

Grammar Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Revise o Present Continuous: ações em andamento, situações temporárias e planos futuros.

Present Continuous — Current Actions, Temporary Situations, Future Arrangements
FORM STRUCTURE EXAMPLE
Affirmative
Afirmativa		Subject + am/is/are + verb-ingMilton is attending a trade show.
Milton está participando de uma feira.
Negative
Negativa		Subject + am/is/are + not + verb-ingHe is not looking for wholesale partners.
Ele não está procurando parceiros de atacado.
Interrogative
Interrogativa		Am/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?Is Milton networking at the show?
Milton está fazendo networking na feira?
Uses
Usos		1. Ações acontecendo AGORA
2. Situações temporárias
3. Planos futuros confirmados		1. "I am showcasing my collection." (now)
2. "We are expanding partnerships." (temporary)
3. "I am meeting suppliers tomorrow." (future)
1. Estou exibindo (agora) / 2. Estamos expandindo (temporário) / 3. Vou me encontrar (futuro)
Atenção: Não confunda Present Simple com Present Continuous:
Present Simple = rotina/fato: "I export jewelry." (sempre)
Present Continuous = agora/temporário: "I am attending a trade show." (esta semana)

✗ "I am export jewelry every day." → ✓ "I export jewelry every day."
✗ "He attends the show right now." → ✓ "He is attending the show right now."

Practice Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Complete as frases com a forma correta do Present Continuous (afirmativa, negativa ou interrogativa).

"Milton a trade show in Miami."
"We our latest collection today."
"Lisa new suppliers."
" Milton networking at the trade show?"
"She the show tomorrow."
"They a follow-up meeting."
"I discussing a partnership."
"Your collection made a great ."

Order Stage 2: Trade Show Conversation Order

Organize as frases na ordem lógica de uma conversa em uma feira de negócios.

?"We are showcasing our latest collection today."
?"Could I schedule a follow-up call next week?"
?"Excuse me, are you exhibiting Brazilian jewelry?"
?"I like your pitch. Do you have a business card?"
?"I am looking for luxury jewelry suppliers for our stores."

Speaking Stage 3: Pronunciation

Ouça cada frase, grave sua voz e compare a pronúncia.

I am attending a jewelry trade show this week.
We are currently expanding our retail partnerships in the U.S.
Here is my business card. Could I schedule a follow-up?
Your collection made a great impression.
I am looking forward to discussing a partnership.

Quiz Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação profissional.

You are at a trade show and someone asks what you are doing here. What do you say?
A "I attend trade shows every year."
B "I am showcasing our latest jewelry collection."
C "I showcase jewelry at this booth."
Someone asks if you are working with department stores. You are not, but you are looking. How do you answer?
A "No, I not working with department stores."
B "Not yet, but we are actively networking to find the right partners."
C "We does not work with stores."
You want to arrange a call for next week. Which is correct?
A "Could I schedule a follow-up call next week?"
B "I am schedule a call to you next week."
C "Can I following up you next week?"
Which sentence correctly uses Present Continuous for a FUTURE arrangement?
A "I meet several suppliers tomorrow."
B "I am meeting several suppliers tomorrow."
C "I will meeting suppliers tomorrow."

Reflection Stage 5: Free Production

Grave sua resposta livremente. Não há certo ou errado — o objetivo é práticar.

Imagine you are at a jewelry trade show in Miami. You meet a potential retail partner. Introduce yourself, describe what you are showcasing, explain what your company is currently doing (expanding, networking), and ask for a follow-up meeting. Use the Present Continuous.

Survival Card — Lesson 3

1I am attending a jewelry trade show this week.Estou participando de uma feira de joias esta semana.
2We are currently expanding our retail partnerships.Estamos atualmente expandindo nossas parcerias de varejo.
3Here is my business card. Could I schedule a follow-up?Aqui está meu cartão. Posso agendar um acompanhamento?
4Your collection made a great impression.Sua coleção causou uma ótima impressão.
5I am looking forward to discussing a partnership.Estou ansioso para discutir uma parceria.

Checklist — Aula 3

  • Estudei as 10 palavras de vocabulário
  • Completei o matching
  • Li o texto de Grammar in Context
  • Revisei o Grammar Tip (Present Continuous)
  • Completei os fill-in-the-blanks
  • Organizei a Trade Show Conversation
  • Pratiquei a pronúncia (5 frases)
  • Respondi o quiz situacional
  • Gravei a produção livre
Aula 04 — Pre-class

Negotiation Essentials

Setting Terms and Closing Deals — Modal Verbs
0%
▼

Vocabulary Warm-up: Lesson 3 Review

Antes de começar, revise 6 palavras da aula anterior.

Networking•Construção de contatos
Building professional relationships
Trade Show•Feira de negócios
An exhibition for a specific industry
Booth•Estande
A stand at a trade show
Prospect•Cliente em potencial
A potential client or partner
Pitch•Apresentação rápida
A short presentation to sell an idea
Follow-up•Acompanhamento
Contact after a meeting to continue

Vocabulary Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Estude cada palavra, ouça a pronúncia e leia o exemplo.

Proposal•Proposta
A formal suggestion or plan
"We sent a proposal to the distributor last week."
Counteroffer•Contraproposta
A response to an offer with different terms
"The client made a counteroffer with different terms."
Terms•Termos / Condições
Conditions of an agreement
"Both parties agreed on the terms of the contract."
Deadline•Prazo
The last date by which something must be done
"The deadline for the delivery is next Friday."
Concession•Concessão
Something given up in a negotiation
"Milton made a concession on the price to close the deal."
Margin•Margem
The difference between cost and selling price
"Our margin on this product is 15%."
Volume•Volume
The quantity of goods traded
"The order volume is 500 units per quarter."
Compromise•Acordo / Ceder
An agreement where both sides give something up
"A good compromise benefits both sides."
Priority•Prioridade
Something that is more important than others
"Delivery speed is a priority for this client."
Agreement•Acordo / Contrato
A decision reached by two or more parties
"They signed the agreement yesterday."

Vocabulary Stage 1.2: Matching

Associe cada palavra em inglês com a tradução correta em português.

Proposal
Counteroffer
Terms
Deadline
Concession
Margin
Volume
Compromise
Priority
Agreement

Grammar Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Leia o texto abaixo. As estruturas gramáticais estão em negrito. Depois, responda as perguntas.

Milton is negotiating with David Chen, a distributor from Texas. David received Milton's proposal last week and would like to discuss the terms.

David asks: "Could you offer a discount for larger volumes?" Milton replies: "I could consider a 5% discount. Would that work for you?"

They also discuss the delivery deadline. David says: "Could we make it 45 days? That is a priority for our stores." Milton makes a concession: "I could adjust the timeline if we agree on the margin."

David thinks this is a fair compromise. He asks: "Should we put this in writing?" Milton agrees: "I would suggest we draft the agreement this week."
1. What does David want to discuss about the proposal?
A He wants to cancel the deal
B He wants to discuss the pricing and terms
C He wants to visit Milton in São Paulo
2. Which modal verb is used for polite suggestions in the text?
A Must
B Could
C Will
3. What concession does Milton make?
A He gives a 50% discount
B He adjusts the delivery timeline if they agree on the margin
C He cancels the deadline

Grammar Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Revise os Modal Verbs para negociação: could, would, should.

Modal Verbs — Polite Proposals, Suggestions, and Advice
MODAL USE EXAMPLE
Could
Poderia		Polite requests and suggestions"Could we discuss the terms?"
"Could you consider a lower price?"
Poderíamos discutir os termos? / Você poderia considerar um preço menor?
Would
Gostaria / Faria		Hypothetical/polite offers"Would you accept a larger volume?"
"I would suggest a 10% discount."
Você aceitaria um volume maior? / Eu sugeriria um desconto de 10%.
Should
Deveria		Advice and recommendations"We should review the deadline."
"You should consider our proposal."
Devemos revisar o prazo. / Você deveria considerar nossa proposta.
Can vs Could: "Can" expressa habilidade, "Could" expressa polidez:
"I can offer 5%." (direto, fato) vs "I could offer 5%." (educado, sugestão)
"Can you lower the price?" (direto) vs "Could you lower the price?" (mais polido)

✗ "Can we discussing the terms?" → ✓ "Could we discuss the terms?" (modal + base form)

Practice Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Complete as frases com o modal verb correto (could, would, should).

" we discuss the terms of this agreement?"
"I suggest a 5% discount for larger volumes."
"We review the delivery deadline."
" you accept a compromise on the margin?"
" you send me the proposal by Friday?"
"You consider our proposal."
"I have them ready by Thursday."
"That sounds like a fair ."

Order Stage 2: Negotiation Conversation Order

Organize as frases na ordem lógica de uma negociação.

?"I could consider a 5% discount for that volume."
?"Should we put this in writing?"
?"Thank you for the proposal. I would like to discuss the pricing."
?"Could we also discuss the delivery deadline?"
?"Could you offer a discount for larger volumes?"

Speaking Stage 3: Pronunciation

Ouça cada frase, grave sua voz e compare a pronúncia.

Could we discuss the terms of this agreement?
I would suggest a 5% discount for larger volumes.
We should review the delivery deadline.
Would you accept a compromise on the margin?
Could you send me the proposal by Friday?

Quiz Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação de negociação.

A distributor wants a lower price. How do you politely suggest a discount?
A "You must accept 5% discount."
B "I could consider a 5% discount for that volume."
C "I can maybe give discount."
You want to recommend that both parties review the deadline. What do you say?
A "We must to review the deadline now."
B "We can reviewing the deadline."
C "We should review the delivery deadline."
You want to politely ask if the other party would accept a compromise. Which is correct?
A "Would you accept a compromise on the margin?"
B "You will accept compromise on margin."
C "Do you can accept a compromise?"
Which sentence correctly uses a modal verb with the base form?
A "Could you offering a discount?"
B "I would to suggest a longer deadline."
C "I would suggest we draft the agreement this week."

Reflection Stage 5: Free Production

Grave sua resposta livremente. Não há certo ou errado — o objetivo é práticar.

Imagine you are negotiating with a new distributor from Texas. Describe: what you would propose, what terms you could offer, what deadline you would suggest, and what compromise you would accept. Use modal verbs: could, would, should.

Survival Card — Lesson 4

1Could we discuss the terms of this agreement?Poderíamos discutir os termos deste acordo?
2I would suggest a 5% discount for larger volumes.Eu sugeriria um desconto de 5% para volumes maiores.
3We should review the delivery deadline.Devemos revisar o prazo de entrega.
4Would you accept a compromise on the margin?Você aceitaria um acordo sobre a margem?
5Could you send me the proposal by Friday?Você poderia me enviar a proposta até sexta?

Checklist — Aula 4

  • Estudei as 10 palavras de vocabulário
  • Completei o matching
  • Li o texto de Grammar in Context
  • Revisei o Grammar Tip (Modal Verbs)
  • Completei os fill-in-the-blanks
  • Organizei a Negotiation Conversation
  • Pratiquei a pronúncia (5 frases)
  • Respondi o quiz situacional
  • Gravei a produção livre
Aula 05 — Pre-class

Board Meetings

Participating and Expressing Opinions — Present Perfect
0%
▼

Vocabulary Warm-up: Lesson 4 Review

Antes de começar, revise 6 palavras da aula anterior.

Proposal•Proposta
A formal suggestion or plan
Counteroffer•Contraproposta
A response to an offer with different terms
Terms•Termos / Condições
Conditions of an agreement
Deadline•Prazo
The last date by which something must be done
Compromise•Acordo / Ceder
An agreement where both sides give something up
Agreement•Acordo / Contrato
A decision reached by two or more parties

Vocabulary Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Estude cada palavra, ouça a pronúncia e leia o exemplo.

Agenda•Pauta / Agenda
A list of topics to discuss at a meeting
"The board reviewed the agenda before the meeting started."
Minutes•Ata
The written record of a meeting
"The secretary sent the minutes to all members."
Motion•Moção
A formal proposal at a meeting
"A tenant made a motion to reduce the assessment fees."
Resolution•Resolução
A formal decision made at a meeting
"The board passed a resolution to increase the budget."
Budget•Orçamento
A financial plan for a period of time
"The maintenance budget has been approved for this year."
Tenant•Inquilino / Condômino
A person who rents a property
"Several tenants have complained about the assessment increase."
Assessment•Avaliação
An evaluation or judgment
"The building needs regular maintenance to stay in good condition."
Maintenance•Manutenção
Keeping a building or equipment in good condition
"The building needs regular maintenance to stay in good condition."
Approve•Aprovar
To officially accept or agree
"The board voted to approve the new safety regulations."
Adjourn•Encerrar (reunião)
To end a meeting temporarily
"The president decided to adjourn the meeting at 9 PM."

Vocabulary Stage 1.2: Matching

Associe cada palavra em inglês com a tradução correta em português.

Agenda
Minutes
Motion
Resolution
Budget
Tenant
Assessment
Maintenance
Approve
Adjourn

Grammar Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Leia o texto abaixo. As estruturas gramáticais estão em negrito. Depois, responda as perguntas.

Milton is at a condo board meeting in Miami. Karen White, the board president, has called the meeting to order. She asks if everyone has received the minutes from the last meeting.

Milton says: "I have read them. I would like to make a motion regarding the assessment fees." He has noticed that maintenance costs have increased by 15% this year.

Karen asks: "Have the other tenants raised concerns?" Milton replies: "Yes, several tenants have expressed their concerns. We have received five complaints this month."

The management company has explained that the increase is due to new safety regulations. Karen suggests they vote on a resolution to approve the revised budget. Milton supports the motion and they plan to adjourn after the vote.
1. What has Milton noticed about the maintenance costs?
A They have decreased by 15%
B They have increased by 15% this year
C They have stayed the same
2. What tense is used in "have received," "has explained," and "have expressed"?
A Past Simple
B Present Perfect
C Present Continuous
3. Why has the management company increased the fees?
A Because the tenants requested it
B Because they want more profit
C Because of new safety regulations

Grammar Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Revise o Present Perfect para experiência e eventos recentes.

Present Perfect — Experience and Recent Events
STRUCTURE USE EXAMPLE
have/has + past participle
Forma afirmativa		Experience / Recent events"I have attended many board meetings."
"Milton has exported jewelry for over thirty years."
Eu participei de muitas reuniões. / Milton exporta joias há mais de trinta anos.
have/has + just + pp
Evento recente		Something that happened very recently"We have just approved the budget."
"The board has decided to increase fees."
Acabamos de aprovar o orçamento. / O conselho decidiu aumentar as taxas.
Have/Has + ever/never
Perguntas de experiência		Asking about life experience"Have you ever presented at a board meeting?"
"I have never missed a meeting."
Você já apresentou em uma reunião? / Eu nunca faltei a uma reunião.
Present Perfect vs Past Simple:
"I attended the meeting yesterday." (tempo específico = Past Simple)
"I have attended many meetings." (experiência geral, sem tempo específico = Present Perfect)

✗ "I have attended the meeting yesterday." → ✓ "I attended the meeting yesterday." (tempo específico = Past Simple)
✗ "I attended many meetings." (sem contexto de tempo) → ✓ "I have attended many meetings." (experiência = Present Perfect)

Practice Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Complete as frases com a forma correta do Present Perfect.

"I the agenda and prepared some notes."
" everyone received the minutes from the last meeting?"
"Several tenants concerns about the fees."
"The board to increase maintenance fees."
"I missed a meeting."
"We five complaints this month."
" you ever presented at a board meeting?"
"Milton jewelry for over thirty years."

Order Stage 2: Board Meeting Sequence

Organize os passos na ordem correta de uma reunião de condomínio.

?"I would like to make a motion regarding the assessment fees."
?"Shall we vote on a resolution to approve the revised budget?"
?"Good evening, everyone. I am calling this meeting to order."
?"Maintenance costs have increased by 15% this year."
?"Has everyone received the minutes from the last meeting?"

Speaking Stage 3: Pronunciation

Ouça cada frase, grave sua voz e compare a pronúncia.

I have reviewed the agenda and prepared some notes.
I would like to make a motion regarding the budget.
Have you received the minutes from the last meeting?
Several tenants have expressed concerns about the fees.
I move to approve the resolution.

Quiz Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação de reunião.

The board president asks if you have reviewed the agenda. How do you respond?
A "I review the agenda yesterday."
B "Yes, I have. I have also prepared some notes."
C "I am reviewing it now."
You want to talk about a recent increase in costs. Which sentence uses the Present Perfect correctly?
A "Maintenance costs have increased by 15% this year."
B "Maintenance costs increased by 15% this year."
C "Maintenance costs are increasing by 15% this year."
You want to ask if someone has ever attended a board meeting. Which is correct?
A "Did you ever attend a board meeting?"
B "Have you ever presented at a board meeting?"
C "Are you ever attending a board meeting?"
Which sentence correctly uses the Present Perfect for a recent event?
A "We have just approved the budget yesterday."
B "We have just approved the budget."
C "We just approve the budget."

Reflection Stage 5: Free Production

Grave sua resposta livremente. Não há certo ou errado — o objetivo é práticar.

Imagine you are at a condo board meeting in Miami. Describe: what items are on the agenda, what motion you would like to make, what the tenants have discussed recently, and whether the board has approved the budget. Use the Present Perfect: have/has + past participle.

Survival Card — Lesson 5

1I have reviewed the agenda and prepared some notes.Eu revisei a pauta e preparei algumas anotações.
2I would like to make a motion regarding the budget.Eu gostaria de fazer uma moção em relação ao orçamento.
3Have you received the minutes from the last meeting?Você recebeu a ata da última reunião?
4Several tenants have expressed concerns about the fees.Vários condôminos expressaram preocupações sobre as taxas.
5I move to approve the resolution.Eu proponho aprovar a resolução.

Checklist — Aula 5

  • Estudei as 10 palavras de vocabulário
  • Completei o matching
  • Li o texto de Grammar in Context
  • Revisei o Grammar Tip (Present Perfect)
  • Completei os fill-in-the-blanks
  • Organizei a Board Meeting Sequence
  • Pratiquei a pronúncia (5 frases)
  • Respondi o quiz situacional
  • Gravei a produção livre
Aula 06 — Pre-class

Email Etiquette

Writing Clear Business Emails — Linking Words
0%
▼
Vocabulary

Stage 1.1: Key Email Vocabulary

Click on each card to listen and learn. These 10 words are essential for professional emails.

Subject Line

The title or topic of an email, shown before opening it.
"A clear subject line helps the recipient understand the purpose of your email."

Recipient

The person who receives the email.
"Make sure the recipient is correct before sending."

Attachment

A file sent together with an email.
"Please find the attachment with the updated contract."

Deadline

The latest date or time by which something must be completed.
"The deadline for the proposal is next Friday."

CC

Carbon Copy — sending a copy of the email to additional people.
"I will CC the legal team on this email."

Follow Up

To contact someone again about a previous topic.
"I am writing to follow up on our previous conversation."

Proofread

To read a text carefully to find and correct mistakes.
"Please proofread the document before sending it."

Regards

A polite closing word in professional emails.
"Best regards, Milton Sayegh."

Forward

To send an email you received to another person.
"Could you forward this email to the sales team?"

Greeting

The opening line of an email, such as "Dear Mr. Smith."
"The greeting sets the tone for a professional email."
Practice

Stage 1.2: Match the Words

Match each word to its definition.

Subject Line
Recipient
Attachment
Deadline
Proofread
Grammar

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Read the text and answer the questions.

Milton writes several business emails every week. Last Tuesday, he needed to contact a new distributor in California. He wrote: "Dear Ms. Chen, I am writing regarding the partnership opportunity we discussed at the trade show. Furthermore, I would like to share our latest catalog. However, the pricing may change after July. Therefore, I recommend confirming the order before the end of this month. In addition, I have attached the updated terms for your review." Milton always proofreads his emails before sending them. He believes that clear, professional communication is the key to successful international business.
1. Why did Milton write to Ms. Chen?
A To cancel a meeting
B To discuss a partnership opportunity
C To complain about delivery
2. What does "Furthermore" introduce in the email?
A A contrast
B Additional information
C A conclusion
3. Why does Milton recommend confirming before month end?
A Because pricing may change after July
B Because he is going on vacation
C Because the catalog is outdated
Grammar

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Linking Words for Business Emails

Linking words connect ideas in emails. They make your writing clear and professional.

FunctionLinking WordExample
AdditionFurthermore / In addition"Furthermore, we offer free shipping."
ContrastHowever / Nevertheless"However, the deadline has changed."
ResultTherefore / As a result"Therefore, I suggest a meeting."
ReferenceRegarding / With regard to"Regarding your inquiry, please see below."
SequenceFirst / Then / Finally"First, let me summarize the terms."
Practice

Stage 1.5: Complete the Sentences

Fill in with the correct linking word.

"I am writing the partnership agreement we discussed last week."
"We have approved the initial order. , we will need to adjust the delivery timeline."
", our quality team will inspect all items before shipment."
"The client has not confirmed the order. , please confirm the shipping address at your earliest convenience."
", I have attached our latest price list for your reference."
Order

Stage 2: Put the Email in Order

Arrange these parts of a business email in the correct order.

?"I am writing regarding the contract we discussed at the trade show."
?"Dear Mr. Rodriguez,"
?"Best regards, Milton Sayegh"
?"I hope this message finds you well."
?"I look forward to hearing from you."
Speaking

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Try to match the rhythm and intonation.

"I am writing to follow up on our previous conversation."
"Please find the attachment with the updated contract."
"I would like to confirm the details of our agreement."
"Furthermore, we need to review the shipping schedule."
"I look forward to hearing from you."
Quiz

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Choose the best option for each situation.

1. You need to send a contract to a new partner. What is the best subject line?
A "Hi"
B "Partnership Agreement — Sayegh Jewelry / Chen Retail"
C "Important!!!"
2. You want to add extra information about shipping. Which linking word is best?
A However
B Therefore
C In addition
3. The client changed the delivery date. You want to introduce this contrast. Which word?
A However
B Furthermore
C Regarding
4. How should you close a formal business email?
A "See ya!"
B "Bye"
C "Best regards, Milton Sayegh"
Reflection

Stage 5: Free Production

Record yourself speaking freely.

Imagine you need to write an email to a new distributor in Los Angeles. Describe what you would write: the greeting, the purpose, and how you would close the email. Use at least 2 linking words (furthermore, however, therefore, regarding, in addition).

Survival Card — Lesson 6

1I am writing regarding the partnership agreement we discussed.
2Please find the attachment with the updated terms.
3Furthermore, I have included the latest catalog for your review.
4Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any questions.
5I look forward to hearing from you.

Checklist — Aula 6

  • I studied the 10 email vocabulary words
  • I completed the matching exercise
  • I read the Grammar in Context text
  • I reviewed the Grammar Tip (Linking Words)
  • I completed the fill-in-the-blanks
  • I organized the email structure
  • I practiced pronunciation (5 phrases)
  • I answered the situational quiz
  • I recorded the free production
Aula 07 - Pre-class

Numbers and Data

Presenting Financial Information - Comparatives & Superlatives
0%
▼
Vocabulary

Stage 1.1: Key Financial Vocabulary

Click on each card to listen and learn. These 10 words are essential for presenting financial results.

Quarter

A three-month period in a company's financial year (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4).
"Our profit increased in the second quarter."

Profit

The money a company keeps after paying all its costs.
"Our profit was higher than last year."

Growth

An increase in size, value, or amount over time.
"We achieved strong growth in the US market."

Forecast

A prediction of future figures or results.
"The forecast for the next quarter is positive."

Figure

A number that represents data or an amount.
"These figures show our best year ever."

Turnover

The total amount of sales in a period.
"Our annual turnover reached twelve million dollars."

Decline

A decrease or fall in figures.
"There was a small decline in European sales."

Exchange Rate

The value of one currency compared to another.
"A better exchange rate increased our profit."

Bottom Line

The final profit figure, after all costs.
"The bottom line is stronger than last year."

Chart

A visual graphic that shows data or numbers.
"This chart compares our four markets."
Practice

Stage 1.2: Match the Words

Match each word to its definition.

Quarter
Profit
Forecast
Turnover
Decline
Grammar

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last week, Milton presented the second-quarter results to his investors. He was proud of the figures. "Our profit this quarter was higher than last year," he said. "In fact, this is the best quarter in our company's history. The US market was more profitable than the European market, and our turnover grew by 18%. However, the exchange rate was the worst of the year, so our costs were slightly higher. Therefore, the forecast for the next quarter is even more positive." Milton's investors were impressed. Clear numbers and confident comparisons made his presentation the strongest of the day.
1. How was the profit this quarter compared to last year?
A Lower than last year
B Higher than last year
C The same as last year
2. Which market was more profitable?
A The US market
B The European market
C The Asian market
3. Why were the costs slightly higher?
A Because of the exchange rate
B Because of new staff
C Because of marketing
Grammar

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip - Comparatives & Superlatives

Use comparatives to compare two things, and superlatives to identify the top one in a group.

FormRuleExample
Comparative - shortadjective + -er than"higher than last year", "lower than Q1"
Comparative - longmore + adjective + than"more profitable than Europe"
Superlative - shortthe + adjective + -est"the highest", "the lowest figure"
Superlative - longthe most + adjective"the most profitable market"
Irregulargood → better → best; bad → worse → worst"better than last year", "the best quarter"

Numbers tip: "rose by 18%", "grew from 1.8 to 2.4 million", "a 15 percent increase". Say "point" for decimals: 2.4 = "two point four".

Practice

Stage 1.5: Complete the Sentences

Fill in with the correct comparative or superlative.

"The second quarter was than the first."
"This is profit in our history."
"The US market is than the European market."
"Our costs are than last year."
"This quarter we had exchange rate of the year."
Order

Stage 2: Open the Presentation

Arrange the opening of a financial presentation in the correct order.

?"First, let me show you the overall figures."
?"Good morning, everyone. Thank you for joining."
?"Now, let's compare the four markets."
?"Today I will present our second-quarter results."
?"As you can see, this was our strongest quarter."
Speaking

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Try to match the rhythm and intonation.

"Our profit this quarter was higher than last year."
"This is the best quarter in our company's history."
"The US market is more profitable than Europe."
"Our turnover grew by eighteen percent."
"The forecast for the next quarter is even stronger."
Quiz

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Choose the best option for each situation.

1. Sales went up 25% this quarter. How do you present it?
A "Sales is more big."
B "Sales increased by twenty-five percent."
C "Sales go up a lot."
2. The US market clearly performed best of all your markets. Which is correct?
A "The US market is the most profitable."
B "The US market is more profitabler."
C "The US market is the most profitablest."
3. Q1 profit was 1.0 million; Q2 profit was 1.5 million. How do you describe Q2?
A "Q2 profit was higher than Q1."
B "Q2 profit was more high than Q1."
C "Q2 profit was the higher."
4. You want to highlight your best-ever annual results. Which phrase?
A "These are gooder figures."
B "These are the highest figures in our history."
C "These figures are the most high."
Reflection

Stage 5: Free Production

Record yourself speaking freely.

Imagine you are presenting your company's results to investors. Compare this year to last year using at least 2 comparatives and 1 superlative. Mention your profit, your growth, and which market was the most profitable.

Survival Card - Lesson 7

1Our profit was higher than last year.
2This is the best quarter in our history.
3The US market is more profitable than Europe.
4Turnover grew by eighteen percent.
5Let me walk you through the figures.

Checklist - Aula 7

  • I studied the 10 financial vocabulary words
  • I completed the matching exercise
  • I read the Grammar in Context text
  • I reviewed the Grammar Tip (comparatives & superlatives)
  • I completed the fill-in-the-blanks
  • I ordered the presentation opening
  • I practiced pronunciation (5 phrases)
  • I answered the situational quiz
  • I recorded the free production
Aula 08 - Pre-class

Review and Consolidation

Lessons 1-7 Together - Tenses, Modals & Comparisons
0%
▼
Vocabulary

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Review

These 8 words from Lessons 1-7 come back today. Click each card to listen and review.

Supplier

A company that provides goods or materials to your business.
"We work with a trusted supplier in Italy."

Client

A person or company that buys your products or services.
"Our biggest client is based in New York."

Deal

A business agreement, especially about buying or selling.
"We closed a great deal at the trade show."

Agenda

A list of the topics to discuss in a meeting.
"The first item on the agenda is the budget."

Deadline

The latest time by which something must be finished.
"The deadline for the order is Friday."

Invoice

A document that lists products sold and the price to pay.
"Please send the invoice to our finance team."

Launch

To introduce a new product to the market.
"We will launch the new collection in March."

Schedule

A plan that shows when things will happen.
"My schedule is full this week."

Forecast

A prediction of future figures or results.
"The forecast for next quarter is positive."
Practice

Stage 1.2: Match the Words

Match each word to its definition.

Supplier
Client
Deal
Agenda
Invoice
Grammar

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Read the text and answer the questions. Notice the different tenses from Lessons 1-7.

Milton manages a luxury jewelry business in Sao Paulo. Every day, he works with suppliers and clients around the world. This week, his team is preparing a new product launch for the US market. "We have worked with our main supplier for over ten years," Milton says. "Last quarter was the best in our history, and our profit was higher than the year before. Could we do even better next year? I believe we can." Right now, Milton is reviewing the agenda for an important board meeting. He has already sent the invoices and confirmed the deadlines.
1. What does Milton do every day?
A He works with suppliers and clients
B He travels to the US
C He designs the products himself
2. How long has Milton worked with his main supplier?
A For one year
B For over ten years
C For six months
3. What is his team doing this week?
A Preparing a new product launch
B Closing the company
C Hiring new staff
Grammar

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip - Tenses Review

Lessons 1-7 covered these key structures. Use the right one for the right situation.

StructureUse it forExample
Present Simpleroutines & facts"I manage an export business."
Present Continuousnow & current projects"We are launching a new line."
Present Perfectexperience & results"I have worked here for 30 years."
Modals (could/would/should)polite proposals"Could you send the invoice?"
Comparatives/Superlativescompare figures"This was the best quarter."

Reminder: Present Simple adds -s for he/she/it ("he works"). Present Perfect uses have/has + past participle ("have been", not "have went").

Practice

Stage 1.5: Complete the Sentences

Fill in with the correct form. Think about which tense fits.

"He the export department."
"Right now, we into the US market."
"I New York three times."
"You confirm the order today."
"This is deal of the year."
Order

Stage 2: Open a Business Meeting

Arrange the opening of a meeting in the correct order.

?"First, let's look at last quarter's results."
?"Good morning, everyone. Thank you for coming."
?"Finally, let's agree on the next steps."
?"Let me share the agenda for today."
?"Then, we will discuss the new launch."
Speaking

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. These review the tenses from Lessons 1-7.

"I manage a jewelry export business."
"We are launching a new collection this month."
"I have worked in this industry for thirty years."
"Could you send me the contract by Friday?"
"This was our best quarter ever."
Quiz

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Choose the best option for each situation from your business day.

1. You meet a new client at a trade show. What do you say?
A "Nice to meet you. I'm Milton, from Sayegh Jewelry."
B "Me Milton. Jewelry."
C "I am the Milton of jewelry."
2. You describe what your company does. Which is correct?
A "We design and export luxury jewelry."
B "We is designing jewelry."
C "We exports a jewelry."
3. You want to propose a price politely in a negotiation. Which is best?
A "Could we agree on a price of fifty dollars per unit?"
B "You give me fifty dollars."
C "Price is fifty, yes?"
4. You present your best-ever results to the board. Which phrase?
A "This is gooder than before."
B "This was our best quarter ever."
C "This is the most best quarter."
Reflection

Stage 5: Free Production

Record yourself speaking freely.

Describe a typical business day for you. Use at least three different structures from Lessons 1-7: a Present Simple fact, a Present Continuous current project, and a Present Perfect experience. Mention a client, a supplier, or a deal.

Survival Card - Lesson 8

1Let me walk you through our process.
2We have worked together for many years.
3I am preparing the report now.
4Could we schedule a meeting next week?
5This is the best option for both of us.

Checklist - Aula 8

  • I reviewed the 8 vocabulary words
  • I completed the matching exercise
  • I read the Grammar in Context text
  • I reviewed the Grammar Tip (tenses review)
  • I completed the fill-in-the-blanks
  • I ordered the meeting opening
  • I practiced pronunciation (5 phrases)
  • I answered the situational quiz
  • I recorded the free production
Aula 09 - Pre-class

Contracts and Agreements

Legal Vocabulary & the Past Simple
0%
▼
Vocabulary

Stage 1.1: Legal & Contract Vocabulary

Click on each card to listen and learn. These 8 words are essential for contracts and agreements.

Contract

A formal written agreement between two parties.
"We signed a contract with our US distributor."

Agreement

An arrangement that both sides accept.
"We reached an agreement after two meetings."

Clause

A specific section or condition in a contract.
"The delivery clause protects both companies."

Terms

The conditions of an agreement.
"We agreed on the terms of payment."

Signature

Your name written to approve a document.
"The contract needs your signature here."

Party

A person or company in a contract.
"Both parties signed the same day."

Obligation

Something you must do under the contract.
"Delivery on time is our main obligation."

Breach

Breaking the terms of a contract.
"A late payment is a breach of the agreement."
Practice

Stage 1.2: Match the Words

Match each word to its definition.

Contract
Clause
Terms
Party
Breach
Grammar

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Read the text and answer the questions. Notice the Past Simple verbs.

Last month, Milton signed an important contract with a US distributor. First, the two parties met in New York and discussed the main terms. Milton's lawyer reviewed every clause carefully. They agreed on the price, the delivery dates, and the payment terms. Then Milton sent the final version by email, and both parties signed it the same week. Nobody breached the agreement, and the first order arrived on time. It was the smoothest deal of the year.
1. What did Milton sign last month?
A A contract with a US distributor
B A new office lease
C An employment contract
2. Who reviewed every clause?
A The distributor
B Milton's lawyer
C The bank
3. Did anyone breach the agreement?
A No, nobody breached it
B Yes, the distributor did
C Yes, Milton did
Grammar

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip - The Past Simple

Use the Past Simple for completed actions in the past (a specific finished time).

FormRuleExample
Regular (+)verb + -ed"We signed / reviewed / agreed."
Irregular (+)special past form"send → sent, meet → met, make → made, pay → paid"
Negative (-)did not (didn't) + base verb"We didn't change the terms."
Question (?)Did + subject + base verb"Did you send the contract?"

After "did" / "didn't", always use the BASE verb (not the past form): "Did you send?" (not "Did you sent?").

Practice

Stage 1.5: Complete the Sentences

Fill in with the correct Past Simple form.

"We the contract yesterday."
"The distributor our terms last week."
"I the email last night."
"They the lawyer on Monday."
"We the terms."
Order

Stage 2: How the Deal Happened

Arrange the steps of the deal in the correct order.

?"Our lawyer reviewed every clause."
?"First, the two parties met in New York."
?"Finally, both parties signed the contract."
?"Then they discussed the main terms."
?"Milton sent the final version by email."
Speaking

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Notice the Past Simple endings.

"We signed a new contract last month."
"I reviewed every clause carefully."
"The two parties agreed on the terms."
"We sent the final version yesterday."
"Did you send the signed copy?"
Quiz

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Choose the best option for each situation.

1. You describe a completed action last week. Which is correct?
A "We signed the contract last week."
B "We sign the contract last week."
C "We have signed last week."
2. You ask a colleague about a past action. Which is correct?
A "Did you send the contract?"
B "Did you sent the contract?"
C "You did send the contract?"
3. You say a past action did NOT happen. Which is correct?
A "We didn't change the terms."
B "We didn't changed the terms."
C "We not changed the terms."
4. A late payment broke the contract conditions. How do you say it?
A "The payment was a clause."
B "The late payment was a breach of the contract."
C "The payment signed the contract."
Reflection

Stage 5: Free Production

Record yourself speaking freely.

Describe the last important deal or contract you closed. Use the Past Simple: when you met the other party, what you discussed, what you agreed, and when you signed. Use at least two irregular verbs (sent, met, made, paid).

Survival Card - Lesson 9

1Let me review the terms of the contract.
2We agreed on the main conditions.
3Could you send me the signed copy?
4Both parties must respect the agreement.
5We signed the contract last week.

Checklist - Aula 9

  • I studied the 8 legal/contract words
  • I completed the matching exercise
  • I read the Grammar in Context text
  • I reviewed the Grammar Tip (Past Simple)
  • I completed the fill-in-the-blanks
  • I ordered the steps of the deal
  • I practiced pronunciation (5 phrases)
  • I answered the situational quiz
  • I recorded the free production
Aula 10 - Pre-class

Supply Chain

From Brazil to the U.S. - the Passive Voice
0%
▼
Vocabulary

Stage 1.1: Supply Chain & Logistics Vocabulary

Click on each card to listen and learn. These 8 words describe how products move from Brazil to the U.S.

Supply Chain

The full process from production to final delivery.
"Our supply chain starts in Brazil."

Shipment

A load of goods sent together.
"The shipment was delivered in twenty days."

Customs

The government office that checks imported goods.
"All cargo is inspected at customs."

Freight

Goods transported in bulk, or the cost of transport.
"Freight by sea is cheaper than by air."

Warehouse

A large building where goods are stored.
"The products are packed in our warehouse."

Cargo

Goods carried by ship, plane, or truck.
"The cargo was inspected at customs."

Logistics

The organization of moving and storing goods.
"Our logistics team manages every shipment."

Container

A large metal box used for shipping goods.
"The containers are sent by sea."
Practice

Stage 1.2: Match the Words

Match each word to its definition.

Shipment
Customs
Warehouse
Cargo
Container
Grammar

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Read the text and answer the questions. Notice the Passive Voice (be + past participle).

At Sayegh Jewelry, every piece is made by hand in Brazil. First, the products are designed in Sao Paulo. Then they are packed carefully in the warehouse. The containers are sent by sea to the United States. Last month, a large shipment was delivered in only twenty days. When the cargo arrived, it was inspected at customs and quickly released. The whole supply chain is managed by an experienced logistics team. Thanks to this process, the jewelry is exported to clients across America.
1. Where is the jewelry made?
A In Brazil
B In the United States
C In Europe
2. How are the containers sent?
A By sea
B By air
C By road
3. What happened to the cargo at customs?
A It was inspected and released
B It was returned to Brazil
C It was lost
Grammar

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip - The Passive Voice

Use the Passive Voice when the action (or the product) is more important than who does it.

FormStructureExample
Present passiveam/is/are + past participle"The jewelry is made in Brazil."
Past passivewas/were + past participle"The order was shipped last week."
With the agent... + by + who/what"It is managed by our team."
Questionbe + subject + past participle"Was the cargo inspected?"

Always use the PAST PARTICIPLE after "be": made, sent, shipped, inspected. "by + agent" is optional - often we don't say who did the action.

Practice

Stage 1.5: Complete the Sentences

Fill in with the correct Passive Voice form.

"Our jewelry in Brazil."
"The order last week."
"Our products to the United States."
"The cargo at customs."
"The containers by sea."
Order

Stage 2: The Journey of a Shipment

Arrange the supply chain steps in the correct order.

?"The containers are sent by sea."
?"The jewelry is made in Brazil."
?"The cargo is inspected at customs."
?"The products are packed in the warehouse."
?"The shipment is delivered to the U.S."
Speaking

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Notice the Passive Voice.

"Our products are made in Brazil."
"The jewelry is exported to the United States."
"The shipment was sent last week."
"The containers are transported by sea."
"Was the cargo checked at customs?"
Quiz

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Choose the best option for each situation.

1. You explain where the products are produced. Which is correct?
A "The jewelry is made in Brazil."
B "The jewelry is make in Brazil."
C "The jewelry makes in Brazil."
2. You say the order was sent in the past. Which is correct?
A "The order was shipped yesterday."
B "The order is shipped yesterday."
C "The order was ship yesterday."
3. You ask if the cargo was checked (past passive question). Which is correct?
A "Was the cargo inspected at customs?"
B "Did the cargo inspected at customs?"
C "Was the cargo inspect at customs?"
4. You describe who manages the process (with the agent). Which is correct?
A "The supply chain is managed by our logistics team."
B "The supply chain is manage by our team."
C "The supply chain manages by our team."
Reflection

Stage 5: Free Production

Record yourself speaking freely.

Describe your company's supply chain, from production to delivery. Use the Passive Voice: where the products are made, how they are packed, how the cargo is shipped, and what happens at customs. Use at least four passive sentences.

Survival Card - Lesson 10

1Our products are made in Brazil.
2The shipment was sent last week.
3How is the cargo transported?
4The shipment is delivered in twenty days.
5Everything was checked at customs.

Checklist - Aula 10

  • I studied the 8 supply chain words
  • I completed the matching exercise
  • I read the Grammar in Context text
  • I reviewed the Grammar Tip (Passive Voice)
  • I completed the fill-in-the-blanks
  • I ordered the supply chain steps
  • I practiced pronunciation (5 phrases)
  • I answered the situational quiz
  • I recorded the free production
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Problem Solving -- When Things Go Wrong

Turn a client complaint into a kept client: name the problem, apologize once with control, and commit to a fix. New words: complaint, defective, faulty, delay, apologize, resolve, inconvenience, refund, replacement, compensation. New structure: the first conditional (if + present, ... will + base) for real, likely fixes, and the second conditional (if + past, ... would + base) for softer advice and offers. PT: transformar uma reclamacao em um cliente mantido -- nomear o problema, pedir desculpas com controle e comprometer-se com uma solucao, usando a primeira e a segunda condicional.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of handling a complaint with control. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario para resolver uma reclamacao com controle.

Complaint -- reclamacao
a statement that something is wrong or not good enough
"We received a complaint about the clasps on three pendants."
Defective -- com defeito
made with a fault, so it does not work as it should
"Three of the twelve pieces were defective."
Faulty -- defeituoso / com falha
not working correctly because of a fault
"The faulty clasps would not close properly."
Delay -- atraso
a situation where something happens later than planned
"A customs hold caused a two-day delay."
Apologize -- pedir desculpas
to say that you are sorry for a problem or mistake
"Let me apologize for the trouble this has caused you."
Resolve -- resolver
to find a clear solution to a problem
"I will resolve this today so the collection still opens on time."
Inconvenience -- transtorno / incomodo
trouble or extra effort a problem causes someone
"I understand this is a real inconvenience before your opening."
Refund -- reembolso
money that is paid back to a buyer for a problem
"If you prefer, we can issue a full refund on the three pieces."
Replacement -- substituicao / peca de reposicao
a new item sent to take the place of a faulty one
"We will ship the replacements on Wednesday."
Compensation -- compensacao
something given to make up for trouble or loss
"As compensation, we would cover the express freight."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Complaint
Defective
Faulty
Delay
Apologize
Resolve
Inconvenience
Refund
Replacement
Compensation

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the call summary and answer the questions. / Leia o resumo da ligacao e responda.

A buyer in New York called with a complaint: three pendants arrived defective, the clasps were faulty, and the collection opened that weekend, so any delay was a serious inconvenience. Milton stayed calm. "Let me apologize for the trouble," he said. Then he committed to a real fix: "If you send the faulty pieces back today, we will ship the replacements on Wednesday." When the buyer hesitated, he advised gently: "If I were you, I would wait for the replacements rather than take a refund." And to keep the relationship, he offered compensation: "To make up for the trouble, we would cover the express freight." He promised to resolve it that day.

1. Why does Milton say "If you send the faulty pieces back today, we will ship..." (not "we would ship")?
A It is a real, likely fix — the first conditional uses present + will.
B "Will" and "would" mean exactly the same thing here.
C The first conditional always uses "would".
2. Why does he switch to "If I were you, I would wait..."?
A Because he is really the buyer.
B It is soft, hypothetical advice — the second conditional uses past + would.
C He made a grammar mistake under pressure.
3. Which sentence is correct after "if" in a real condition?
A "If you will return them, we will refund you."
B "If you return them, we will refund you."
C "If you returning them, we will refund you."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- First & Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Use the first conditional for real fixes, the second for softer offers and advice.

TypeStructureExample
First (real)If + present simple, ... will + base verb"If you confirm now, we will start tonight."
Second (hypothetical)If + past simple, ... would + base verb"If I were you, I would keep the order."
Word orderResult first → no comma"We will resolve this if you confirm now."
Watch the traps: after "if" in a real condition, use the present simple, NOT "will" ("if you send", not "if you will send"); in the second conditional use the fixed form "were" for all subjects ("if I were you"); "will" = a real promise, "would" = a softer, more polite offer. PT: use a PRIMEIRA condicional (if + presente, ... will + verbo) para solucoes reais e provaveis -- "Se voce confirmar agora, comecaremos hoje a noite." Use a SEGUNDA condicional (if + passado, ... would + verbo) para conselhos e ofertas mais suaves -- "Se eu fosse voce, ficaria com o pedido." Nunca use "will" depois de "if" numa condicao real; e use sempre "were" (nao "was") na segunda condicional.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each conditional. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada condicional.

"If you confirm the order today, we start the replacements tonight."
"If I you, I would keep the rest of the order."
"We will cover the freight if the delay on us."
"If we offered a full refund, you lose the pieces for the opening."
"If the clasps again, we will replace the whole batch."

Stage 2: Put the Response in Order

Order

Put the steps of a strong complaint response in order. / Coloque os passos de uma boa resposta a reclamacao em ordem.

?Name the problem clearly: what is defective or faulty.
?Close with a calm commitment and offer compensation.
?Thank the client for flagging the issue.
?Promise a dated fix with a first conditional.
?Apologize once, calmly, for the inconvenience.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

Thank you for flagging this — let me apologize for the inconvenience.
Obrigado por avisar — deixe-me pedir desculpas pelo transtorno.
If you send the faulty pieces back today, we will ship replacements on Wednesday.
Se voce devolver as pecas com defeito hoje, enviaremos as substituicoes na quarta.
If I were you, I would wait for the replacement.
Se eu fosse voce, esperaria pela substituicao.
To make up for the trouble, we would cover the express freight.
Para compensar o transtorno, cobririamos o frete expresso.
You have my word: I will resolve this today.
Tem a minha palavra: vou resolver isso hoje.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

A client calls about defective pendants the day before an opening. How do you start?
A Explain at length why it was not your fault.
B Thank them, apologize once for the inconvenience, then move to the fix.
C Promise nothing until you investigate for a week.
You can deliver replacements by Wednesday. Which sentence is correct?
A "If you will return them today, we will ship Wednesday."
B "If you return them today, we will ship Wednesday."
C "If you return them today, we would ship Wednesday."
The client is unsure about keeping the order. You want to advise gently. Which is best?
