A "You must keep the order."
B "If I were you, I would wait for the replacement."
C "If I was you, I will wait for the replacement."
You want to keep the relationship after the trouble. What do you offer?
A Nothing — the replacement is enough on its own.
B Compensation: "To make up for the trouble, we would cover the express freight."
C A discount you cannot actually deliver.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute response to a real client complaint about a jewelry order. Thank the client, apologize once for the inconvenience, name the problem (defective / faulty / delay), promise a dated fix with a first conditional, and offer a replacement, refund, or compensation. Use at least four words from the toolkit. / Grave dois minutos respondendo a uma reclamacao real.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Thank you for flagging this — let me apologize for the inconvenience.Obrigado por avisar — deixe-me pedir desculpas pelo transtorno.
2If you return them today, we will ship replacements on Wednesday.Se voce devolver hoje, enviaremos as substituicoes na quarta.
3If I were you, I would wait for the replacement.Se eu fosse voce, esperaria pela substituicao.
4To make up for the trouble, we would cover the express freight.Para compensar o transtorno, cobririamos o frete expresso.
5You have my word: I will resolve this today.Tem a minha palavra: vou resolver isso hoje.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Cultural Intelligence -- Brazilian vs American Business

Read the room across cultures: punctuality, small talk, directness, hierarchy and rapport. New words: etiquette, punctuality, small talk, directness, hierarchy, rapport, formality, courtesy, assertive, networking. New structure: modals of advice and expectation -- should and ought to (advice), be supposed to (an expected rule), and had better (advice with a warning). PT: ler o ambiente entre culturas -- pontualidade, conversa breve, franqueza e construcao de confianca, usando modais de conselho e expectativa (should, ought to, be supposed to, had better).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of comparing business cultures. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario para comparar culturas de negocios.

Etiquette -- etiqueta / regras de conduta
the accepted rules for polite behavior in a place or job
"Business etiquette in New York rewards speed and clarity."
Punctuality -- pontualidade
the habit of arriving or starting exactly on time
"In the U.S., punctuality is a sign of respect."
Small talk -- conversa breve / amena
light, friendly conversation before the real business starts
"Two minutes of small talk, then they open the laptops."
Directness -- franqueza / objetividade
saying what you think clearly, without softening it too much
"Americans read directness as honesty, not rudeness."
Hierarchy -- hierarquia
the order of levels of authority, from the top down
"In flatter U.S. teams, hierarchy is less visible in the room."
Rapport -- sintonia / boa relacao
a friendly, trusting connection between two people
"He built rapport with the buyer before talking price."
Formality -- formalidade
the level of polite, official behavior a situation calls for
"A first meeting needs more formality than a follow-up call."
Courtesy -- cortesia / gentileza
polite, considerate behavior that shows respect
"A short thank-you note is a simple courtesy that builds trust."
Assertive -- assertivo / firme
confident and direct in a calm, respectful way
"Be assertive about your price without being aggressive."
Networking -- criar uma rede de contatos
building useful professional contacts and relationships
"The fair was great for networking with U.S. retailers."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Etiquette
Punctuality
Small talk
Directness
Hierarchy
Rapport
Formality
Courtesy
Assertive
Networking

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the briefing and answer the questions. / Leia o briefing e responda.

Before a trip to New York, a buyer briefed Milton on local etiquette. "Punctuality is strict," she said. "You are supposed to be seated when the meeting starts, so you should arrive five minutes early." She explained that the small talk is short, and that directness is read as courtesy, not rudeness: "You ought to state your price clearly." Then a warning: "You had better not undersell your work — be assertive about its value." She added that hierarchy is less visible there, and that good networking turns into real rapport only if you follow up fast.

1. Why does she say "You are supposed to be seated" (not "you should be seated")?
A It is an expected cultural rule, not just her personal advice.
B "Supposed to" and "should" mean exactly the same thing.
C "Supposed to" is only used for the past.
2. What does "You had better not undersell your work" add to the advice?
A Nothing — it is the same as "you can undersell".
B A warning — there is a bad consequence if he ignores it.
C A polite request for permission.
3. Which sentence has the correct form?
A "You should to arrive early."
B "You ought to arrive early."
C "You had better to arrive early."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modals of Advice & Expectation

GRAMMAR

Use should / ought to for advice, be supposed to for an expected rule, and had better for advice with a warning.

FormMeaningExample
should / ought to + baseAdvice"You should confirm the time." / "You ought to be early."
be supposed to + baseExpected rule"You're supposed to RSVP before Friday." (the norm)
had better (not) + baseAdvice + warning"You'd better not be late — they will notice."
Watch the traps: after "should" and "had better" use the base verb with NO "to" ("you should go", not "should to go"). "ought TO" and "supposed TO" keep the "to". "should/ought to" = a good idea; "be supposed to" = what is expected of you; "had better" = strong advice with an implied consequence. PT: use SHOULD / OUGHT TO para conselho ("Voce deveria chegar cedo"); BE SUPPOSED TO para uma regra esperada ("Voce deve / e esperado que confirme presenca ate sexta"); HAD BETTER para conselho com aviso ("E melhor voce nao se atrasar -- eles vao reparar"). Nunca use "to" depois de should ou had better; mas mantenha o "to" em ought to e supposed to.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each piece of advice. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada conselho.

"You arrive five minutes early for the meeting."
"You are to confirm your attendance by Friday."
"You had not undersell your work in the room."
"You ought keep the small talk short with American buyers."
"You should be about your price without being aggressive."

Stage 2: Put the Briefing in Order

Order

Put the steps of briefing a colleague for a U.S. meeting in order. / Coloque os passos do briefing em ordem.

?Explain that small talk is short, then they move to numbers.
?Warn them not to undersell the work, and to follow up fast.
?Start with the most important rule: strict punctuality.
?Reassure them that directness reads as courtesy, not rudeness.
?Tell them they're supposed to be seated when it starts, so arrive early.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

Thank you for making time — I'll be brief.
Obrigado por reservar um tempo — serei breve.
You should arrive five minutes early.
Voce deveria chegar cinco minutos mais cedo.
You are supposed to be seated when the meeting starts.
Espera-se que voce esteja sentado quando a reuniao comecar.
You had better not undersell your work.
E melhor voce nao subvalorizar o seu trabalho.
Let me be direct: at that price, the margin doesn't work.
Vou ser direto: nesse preco, a margem nao fecha.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

A colleague asks how strict timing is for a New York meeting. What do you say?
A "Don't worry, people drift in over the first twenty minutes."
B "You're supposed to be seated when it starts, so you should arrive early."
C "Punctuality doesn't matter much there."
Which sentence has the correct form?
A "You should to keep the small talk short."
B "You should keep the small talk short."
C "You had better to keep the small talk short."
Your colleague feels rude stating a price firmly. How do you reassure them?
A "Yes, better stay quiet and hope they offer more."
B "Be assertive — here directness reads as courtesy, not rudeness."
C "Never mention the price at all."
After a trade fair, what is the expected next step with new contacts?
A Wait a few months and hope they remember you.
B "You're supposed to follow up fast — that's how networking becomes rapport."
C Never contact them again to avoid seeming pushy.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute briefing for a Brazilian colleague going to their first U.S. business meeting. Cover punctuality, small talk, directness, and confidence. Use should, ought to, be supposed to, and had better at least once each, and at least four words from the toolkit. / Grave dois minutos de briefing para um colega brasileiro.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Thank you for making time — I'll be brief.Obrigado por reservar um tempo — serei breve.
2Let me be direct: at that price, the margin doesn't work.Vou ser direto: nesse preco, a margem nao fecha.
3Our work speaks for itself — let me show you why.Nosso trabalho fala por si — deixe-me mostrar por que.
4I appreciate the warm welcome — shall we get started?Agradeco a calorosa recepcao — vamos comecar?
5It was great connecting — I'd love to stay in touch.Foi otimo nos conectarmos — adoraria manter contato.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Closing the Deal -- Negotiating Price & Terms

Negotiate price and terms like a pro: ballpark figures, trade-offs, win-win deals, leverage and closing. New words: negotiate, ballpark, win-win, trade-off, leverage, deadlock, bargain, markup, installment, settle. New structure: polite and indirect questions -- "Could you tell me...", "Do you know...", "Would you mind + -ing", "I was wondering if..." -- with statement word order and no "do/does/did" inside. PT: negociar preco e condicoes -- estimativa, troca, acordo vantajoso e fechamento -- pedindo o que voce quer com perguntas polidas e indiretas.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of the negotiating table. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario da mesa de negociacao.

Negotiate -- negociar
to discuss the terms of a deal until both sides reach an agreement
"We still need to negotiate the price for the spring order."
Ballpark -- estimativa aproximada / faixa de preco
an approximate figure or price range, not the exact number yet
"Could you give me a ballpark for the spring order?"
Win-win -- vantajoso para os dois lados
a deal in which both sides gain something and leave satisfied
"A smaller discount but a bigger order — that's win-win."
Trade-off -- troca / contrapartida
when you give up one thing to get another in return
"A longer deadline for a better price is a fair trade-off."
Leverage -- vantagem / poder de barganha
an advantage that gives you power in a negotiation
"A strong waiting list gives us leverage on price."
Deadlock -- impasse
a point where neither side will move and nothing progresses
"We hit a deadlock over the discount, so we paused for a day."
Bargain -- pechincha / negociar preco
a purchase at a good price; also, to discuss a price to make it lower
"At that volume, the necklace is a real bargain for them."
Markup -- margem / acrescimo sobre o custo
the amount added to the cost of a product to set its selling price
"Luxury retail carries a high markup from factory to shelf."
Installment -- parcela / pagamento parcelado
one of several equal payments made over time instead of all at once
"They paid in three installments over ninety days."
Settle -- fechar / chegar a um acordo
to reach a final agreement and end the negotiation
"I think we can settle this today and both walk away happy."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Negotiate
Ballpark
Win-win
Trade-off
Leverage
Deadlock
Bargain
Markup
Installment
Settle

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the negotiation and answer the questions. / Leia a negociacao e responda.

A buyer wanted to negotiate the price on Milton's collection. "Your markup is high for our floor," she said, and made a counteroffer of twelve percent off. Milton asked softly, "Could you tell me what volume you can commit to?" — not "what volume can you commit". He had some leverage, since the pieces were a real bargain at that quality, so he replied, "I was wondering if eight percent would work." To avoid a deadlock, they found a compromise: ten percent, with one concession on payment — three installments. "Would you mind settling the rest on delivery?" he asked. They agreed and decided to settle the deal that day.

1. Why does Milton say "Could you tell me what volume you can commit to?" (not "what volume can you commit")?
A Inside an indirect question, you keep statement order (subject + verb).
B Both versions are wrong.
C "Could you tell me" must be followed by inversion.
2. What follows "Would you mind"?
A The base verb with "to" (mind to settle).
B A verb + -ing (mind settling).
C A noun only, never a verb.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Do you know when do they decide?"
B "Do you know when they decide?"
C "I was wondering you could accept installments."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite & Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

Soften a question with an opener, then keep statement order with no "do/does/did".

OpenerWhat followsExample
Could you tell me... / Do you know...subject + verb (statement order)"Could you tell me what the markup is?"
Would you mind...verb + -ing"Would you mind lowering the price a little?"
I was wondering if...subject + could / would + base"I was wondering if you could accept installments."
Watch the traps: inside an indirect question, keep statement order and drop "do/does/did": "Do you know when they decide?" (NOT "when do they decide"). "Could you tell me what the price is?" (NOT "what is the price"). After "Would you mind", use -ing (mind lowering, not mind to lower). After "I was wondering", you need "if". PT: amacie a pergunta com um inicio polido ("Could you tell me...", "I was wondering if...") e mantenha a ORDEM DE FRASE AFIRMATIVA dentro (sujeito + verbo), sem "do/does/did" e sem inversao. Depois de "Would you mind", use o verbo com -ING.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each polite question or negotiation line. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada frase.

"Could you tell me what the markup ?"
"Would you lowering the price a little?"
"I was wondering you could accept installments."
"Could you give me a for the spring order?"
"Let's not reach a ; we can settle this today."

Stage 2: Put the Negotiation in Order

Order

Put the steps of a price negotiation in order. / Coloque os passos da negociacao em ordem.

?Make a lower counteroffer, framed as a polite indirect question.
?Avoid a deadlock and settle the deal.
?Listen to the buyer's counteroffer on price.
?Offer one concession (installments) to find a compromise.
?Ask about their volume to understand your leverage.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

Could you tell me how much room you need on price?
Voce poderia me dizer quanta margem precisa no preco?
I was wondering if eight percent would work.
Eu queria saber se oito por cento funcionaria.
Would you mind splitting it into three installments?
Voce se importaria de dividir em tres parcelas?
Let's not reach a deadlock — we're close.
Vamos evitar um impasse — estamos perto.
I think we can settle this today and both walk away happy.
Acho que podemos fechar isso hoje e os dois sairem satisfeitos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

A buyer asks for fifteen percent off. How do you reply politely?
A "No, that's impossible, take it or leave it."
B "That's a real concession to ask for — I was wondering if eight would work."
C "Fine, fifteen it is."
Which polite question has the correct form?
A "Could you tell me what is the discount?"
B "Could you tell me what the discount is?"
C "Could you tell me what does the discount?"
You'd rather give better terms than a deeper discount. What do you propose?
A "Would you mind to pay in installments?"
B "Would you be open to three installments instead of a bigger discount?"
C "Pay now or no deal."
The talk is stuck on the last point. How do you keep it alive?
A "Then there is no deal."
B "Let's not reach a deadlock over this — we can settle it today."
C "I'll call you in a few months."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute negotiation for one of your pieces with a tough buyer. Open with a polite indirect question, make a counteroffer, offer one concession, avoid a deadlock, and settle. Use at least four words from the toolkit, and at least one "Could you tell me...", "Would you mind...", and "I was wondering if...". / Grave dois minutos de negociacao com um comprador exigente.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1Could you tell me how much room you need on price?Voce poderia me dizer quanta margem precisa no preco?
2That's a real concession — I was wondering if eight would work.Isso e uma concessao e tanto — eu queria saber se oito funcionaria.
3Would you be open to three installments instead?Voce estaria aberto a tres parcelas no lugar disso?
4Let's not reach a deadlock — we're close.Vamos evitar um impasse — estamos perto.
5I think we can settle this today and both walk away happy.Acho que podemos fechar isso hoje e os dois sairem satisfeitos.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

After the Deal -- Delivery & Keeping the Client

Run everything after the handshake: confirm the invoice and paperwork, track the shipment, hit each milestone, handle after-sales and onboard a client you want to retain. New words: invoice, shipment, lead time, deliverable, milestone, after-sales, onboard, retain, paperwork, fulfill. New structure: future forms -- "will" for a promise decided now, "be going to" for an existing plan, and the present continuous for a fixed arrangement. PT: cuide de tudo depois do acordo -- nota fiscal, envio, marcos, pos-venda -- falando do futuro com will, be going to e present continuous.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of everything after the deal. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario de tudo que vem depois do acordo.

Invoice -- nota fiscal / fatura
a document that lists the goods, the price, and what the client must pay
"I'll email the invoice the day after we sign."
Shipment -- remessa / carga enviada
a batch of goods sent from the seller to the buyer
"The first shipment leaves São Paulo on Monday."
Lead time -- prazo de entrega
the time it takes from placing an order to delivering it
"Our lead time on a custom piece is three weeks."
Deliverable -- item a ser entregue / entrega
a specific item or result you have promised to provide
"The deliverable is forty necklaces, boxed and labeled."
Milestone -- marco / etapa-chave
an important point or target along the way to finishing something
"Their store opening is the milestone we must hit."
After-sales -- pos-venda
the care and support you give a client once the deal is done
"In luxury, after-sales is what keeps the client loyal."
Onboard -- integrar / orientar (cliente novo)
to guide a new client or team so they can use what you provide
"We'll onboard their staff on how to display the pieces."
Retain -- reter / manter (o cliente)
to keep a client over time, so they come back and buy again
"If we deliver well, we retain them for years."
Paperwork -- documentacao / papelada
the documents and forms needed to make a deal or order official
"I'll send the signed paperwork this afternoon."
Fulfill -- cumprir / honrar (uma promessa)
to carry out and complete a promise, order, or obligation
"If we fulfill every promise, the client stays."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Invoice
Shipment
Lead time
Deliverable
Milestone
After-sales
Onboard
Retain
Paperwork
Fulfill

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the handover and answer the questions. / Leia o handover e responda.

The deal was signed, so Milton moved to the handover. "I'll send the invoice and the paperwork today," he promised on the spot. The shipping slot was already booked, so he said, "We're shipping the first shipment on Monday, on the lead time we quoted." He already had a plan for the buyer's team: "We're going to onboard them the week the deliverable arrives." The store opening was the milestone, so the goods had to land before it. For after-sales, he added a promise: "I'll call you a week in." If they fulfill every promise, he was sure they would retain the client for years.

1. Milton says "I'll send the invoice today" on the spot. Why "will"?
A "Will" fits a promise or offer decided in the moment.
B "Will" is only for fixed, booked times.
C "Will" means the plan was made last week.
2. The shipping slot is booked for Monday. Which form does he use?
A "will ship" -- because it is the future.
B "We're shipping" -- present continuous for a fixed arrangement.
C "We ship" -- present simple for a one-time plan.
3. He already planned the onboarding. Which sentence is correct?
A "We going to onboard them next week."
B "We're going to onboard them next week."
C "We will going to onboard them next week."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Future Forms (will / be going to / present continuous)

GRAMMAR

Same future, three uses: a promise decided now, a plan you already have, and a fixed arrangement.

FormUse it forExample
will + basea promise, offer, or prediction decided NOW"I'll send the invoice today."
be going to + basea plan or intention you ALREADY have"We're going to onboard their team."
present continuous (am/is/are + -ing)a fixed arrangement, usually with a time"We're shipping on Monday at nine."
Watch the traps: for a booked, scheduled time use the present continuous, not "will" ("We're shipping Monday", NOT "We will ship Monday"). Don't drop the "be": "We're going to onboard" (NOT "We going to"). PT: o mesmo futuro tem tres usos -- "will" para promessa/oferta decidida na hora; "be going to" para um plano que voce JA tem; e o present continuous (am/is/are + -ING) para um compromisso ja marcado, geralmente com hora. Nao esqueca o "be".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each handover line with the right future form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada frase.

"I send the invoice and the paperwork today."
"We're the first batch on Monday."
"We're going onboard your team after delivery."
"Our on a custom piece is three weeks."
"If we every promise, we'll retain the client."

Stage 2: Put the Handover in Order

Order

Put the steps of an after-deal handover in order. / Coloque os passos do handover em ordem.

?Ship the first batch on the lead time you quoted.
?Call for after-sales and fulfill every promise to retain the client.
?Send the invoice and the signed paperwork.
?Onboard the team the week the deliverable arrives.
?Confirm the milestone date so the shipment lands before it.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

I'll send the invoice and the paperwork today.
Vou enviar a nota fiscal e a documentacao hoje.
We're shipping the first batch on the lead time we quoted.
Vamos enviar o primeiro lote no prazo que combinamos.
We're going to onboard your team after delivery.
Vamos integrar a sua equipe depois da entrega.
I'll call you a week after delivery for after-sales.
Vou ligar uma semana apos a entrega para o pos-venda.
If we fulfill every promise, we'll retain them for years.
Se cumprirmos cada promessa, vamos manter o cliente por anos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

The client asks for the invoice right now. How do you reply?
A "I am sending it last week."
B "I'll send the invoice and the paperwork right away."
C "Maybe the invoice comes someday."
The shipping date is already booked for Monday. Which is most natural?
A "We will ship the batch on Monday." (it's already fixed)
B "We're shipping the batch on Monday."
C "We ship the batch on Monday someday."
You already planned to onboard the client's staff. What do you say?
A "We going to onboard your staff next week."
B "We're going to onboard your staff next week."
C "We will going to onboard your staff next week."
You want to reassure the client about after-sales. What is best?
A "After the sale, you are on your own."
B "I'll call you a week after delivery, and we'll fulfill every promise to retain you."
C "We never talk again after delivery."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute handover message for a client right after a signed deal. Promise the invoice and paperwork, confirm the booked shipment, name the milestone, plan the onboarding, and promise an after-sales call. Use at least four words from the toolkit, and at least one "will", one "be going to", and one present continuous arrangement. / Grave dois minutos de mensagem de handover apos um acordo assinado.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1I'll send the invoice and the paperwork today.Vou enviar a nota fiscal e a documentacao hoje.
2We're shipping the first batch on the lead time we quoted.Vamos enviar o primeiro lote no prazo que combinamos.
3We're going to onboard your team after delivery.Vamos integrar a sua equipe depois da entrega.
4I'll call you a week after delivery for after-sales.Vou ligar uma semana apos a entrega para o pos-venda.
5If we fulfill every promise, we'll retain you for years.Se cumprirmos cada promessa, vamos manter voce por anos.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Handling Problems After Delivery

When something goes wrong after the goods arrive, the relationship is on the line. Acknowledge the issue, reassure the client, escalate it to the right person, sort out a discrepancy, reimburse and reinstate, and protect the goodwill you built. New words: acknowledge, reassure, escalate, discrepancy, reimburse, reinstate, damaged in transit, liability, goodwill, outstanding. New structure: reported speech -- relay what a client said to your team (she said the box was damaged; he told me he would reimburse us). PT: quando algo da errado apos a entrega, salve a relacao -- admita, tranquilize, escale e conserte -- e relate ao time com reported speech.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of handling a problem after delivery. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario de lidar com um problema depois da entrega.

Acknowledge -- reconhecer / admitir
to admit openly that something is true, often a problem
"I acknowledge the box arrived damaged, and I'm sorry."
Reassure -- tranquilizar / dar seguranca
to say something that makes a worried person feel calm and confident
"Let me reassure you: we'll make this right quickly."
Escalate -- escalar / encaminhar a um superior
to pass a problem to a more senior person so it gets solved faster
"I'll escalate this to me directly, not the queue."
Discrepancy -- divergencia / inconsistencia
a difference between two things that should match, like an order and a delivery
"There's a discrepancy between the order and the shipment."
Reimburse -- ressarcir / reembolsar
to pay someone back for money they lost or spent
"We'll reimburse you for the two damaged pieces."
Reinstate -- repor / restabelecer
to bring back or restore something that was removed or missing
"We'll reinstate the missing bracelet on the next shipment."
Damaged in transit -- danificado no transporte
harmed while being transported from the seller to the buyer
"Two boxes were damaged in transit, not at our workshop."
Liability -- responsabilidade (legal)
legal responsibility for a problem, cost, or harm
"Once it ships, the liability for transit damage is the carrier's."
Goodwill -- boa vontade / gesto de cortesia
kind feeling and trust between people, often shown by a generous gesture
"We'll cover the cost as a goodwill gesture."
Outstanding -- pendente / nao resolvido
still unresolved or not yet dealt with
"While the issue is outstanding, I'll update you daily."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Acknowledge
Reassure
Escalate
Discrepancy
Reimburse
Reinstate
Damaged in transit
Liability
Goodwill
Outstanding

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read how Milton reports the problem to his team, then answer. / Leia como o Milton relata o problema ao time e responda.

Karen called with a problem, so Milton briefed his team. He told them that two boxes were damaged in transit and that one bracelet was missing. She said there was a clear discrepancy between the order and what arrived, and that she had checked the paperwork twice. Milton explained that he would acknowledge the issue honestly and reassure her, then escalate it to himself. He promised her he would reimburse the damaged pieces and reinstate the missing one, covering it as a goodwill gesture while it was outstanding. The liability for transit damage, he noted, sat with the carrier.

1. Karen said: "The bracelet is missing." How does Milton report it?
A He said the bracelet is missing.
B He said the bracelet was missing. (present → past)
C He said the bracelet will be missing.
2. Which sentence uses "tell" correctly?
A She told that there was a discrepancy.
B She told me there was a discrepancy. (tell + a person)
C She said me there was a discrepancy.
3. Karen said: "I will send photos." How does Milton report it?
A She said she will send photos.
B She said she would send photos. (will → would)
C She said she sends photos.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech (say / tell + backshift)

GRAMMAR

When you relay what someone said, move the verb one step back in time, and choose say or tell.

Direct speechReported speechExample
present (is / are)past (was / were)"It's damaged." → She said it was damaged.
past simple (arrived)past perfect (had arrived)"It arrived broken." → She said it had arrived broken.
will / canwould / could"I'll send photos." → She said she would send photos.
say vs tell: use tell + a person (she told me) and say without one (she said). PT: ao relatar a fala de alguem, volte o verbo um passo no tempo -- presente vira passado, passado simples vira past perfect, will vira would, can vira could. Use "tell" com a pessoa (she told me) e "say" sem ela (she said). Nao diga "say me".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Report each line or choose the right word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada frase.

Karen: "The box is damaged." → She said the box damaged.
Karen: "I'll send photos." → She me she would send photos.
"Thank you for telling me. I the problem and I'm sorry."
"We'll the damaged pieces and reinstate the missing one."
"We'll cover the cost as a gesture while it's outstanding."

Stage 2: Put the Resolution in Order

Order

Put the steps of handling a complaint in order. / Coloque os passos de resolver uma reclamacao em ordem.

?Escalate the issue to the right person so it isn't buried.
?Cover the cost as goodwill and keep the client updated.
?Thank the client and acknowledge the problem honestly.
?Reimburse the damaged pieces and reinstate the missing one.
?Reassure the client and name the discrepancy clearly.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

Thank you for telling me. I acknowledge the problem.
Obrigado por me avisar. Eu reconheco o problema.
Let me reassure you -- we'll sort out the discrepancy today.
Deixe-me tranquiliza-lo: vamos resolver a divergencia hoje.
I'm escalating this to me directly so it isn't buried in a queue.
Estou escalando isso direto para mim, para nao ficar perdido na fila.
We'll reimburse the damaged pieces and reinstate the missing one.
Vamos ressarcir as pecas danificadas e repor a que faltou.
She told me the box was damaged, so we'll cover it as goodwill.
Ela me disse que a caixa estava danificada, entao vamos cobrir como cortesia.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

A client calls: a piece arrived damaged. What do you say first?
A "That's not our fault, talk to the carrier."
B "Thank you for telling me. I acknowledge the problem, and let me reassure you we'll make it right."
C "Are you sure it was really damaged?"
You need to report the client's words to your team. Which is correct?
A "She said me the shipment is late."
B "She told me the shipment was late."
C "She told the shipment is late."
There's a gap between the order and what arrived. How do you handle it?
A "Let's ignore the discrepancy for now."
B "There's a discrepancy, so I'm escalating it to me directly."
C "A discrepancy is your problem, not mine."
You want to protect the relationship while the issue is unresolved. What's best?
A "We'll deal with it after the next order."
B "We'll reimburse you and cover the cost as a goodwill gesture while it's outstanding."
C "Once it ships, the liability is yours, so there's nothing we can do."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute message to your team reporting a client complaint and your plan. First, relay what the client said using reported speech (she said..., she told me..., she would...). Then say how you'll handle it: acknowledge, reassure, escalate, and offer a concrete fix (reimburse, reinstate, goodwill). Use at least four words from the toolkit. / Grave dois minutos relatando ao seu time uma reclamacao e o plano, usando reported speech.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Thank you for telling me. I acknowledge the problem.Obrigado por me avisar. Eu reconheco o problema.
2Let me reassure you -- we'll sort out the discrepancy today.Deixe-me tranquiliza-lo: vamos resolver a divergencia hoje.
3I'm escalating this to me directly.Estou escalando isso direto para mim.
4We'll reimburse the damaged pieces and reinstate the missing one.Vamos ressarcir as pecas danificadas e repor a que faltou.
5She told me the box was damaged, so we'll cover it as goodwill.Ela me disse que a caixa estava danificada, entao vamos cobrir como cortesia.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Review & Consolidation -- The Business Lunch

A checkpoint over a celebratory lunch with Karen. Put together everything from Lessons 9-15: problem solving with conditionals, cultural intelligence with modals, polite indirect questions, future plans (will / going to) and reporting back with reported speech -- while you host a meal, recommend a dish, raise a toast and reflect on your progress. New social words: host, treat, recommend, appetizer, toast, recap, takeaway, confident. PT: uma aula de revisao -- junte tudo dos ultimos seis encontros em uma unica conversa de almoco e reflita sobre o quanto seu ingles evoluiu.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The friendly language of hosting a business lunch -- and of reflecting on your year. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario de receber alguem em um almoco de negocios e de refletir sobre o ano.

Host -- receber / ser anfitriao
to invite someone and take care of them, often paying for the meal
"Let me host you this time -- you hosted me in New York."
Treat -- pagar / oferecer (como cortesia)
to pay for someone's meal or drink as a generous gesture
"Please, this lunch is my treat."
Recommend -- recomendar / sugerir
to suggest something because you believe it is good
"I'd recommend the risotto -- it's their best dish."
Appetizer -- entrada (prato)
a small dish you eat at the start of a meal, before the main course
"Shall we share a couple of appetizers to start?"
Toast -- brinde
to raise your glass and say a few words to celebrate or wish someone well
"Let's raise a toast to a great year together."
Recap -- recapitular / resumir
to summarize the main points of something briefly
"Let me recap what we've built this year."
Takeaway -- licao principal / o que se leva
the main point or lesson you remember and keep from an experience
"My biggest takeaway this year is confidence."
Confident -- confiante / seguro
feeling sure of yourself and of what you can do
"I feel confident speaking English in any meeting now."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Host
Treat
Recommend
Appetizer
Toast
Recap
Takeaway
Confident

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read how Milton recaps his year, then answer. Notice the structures from Lessons 9-15. / Leia como o Milton recapitula o ano e responda; observe as estruturas das Licoes 9-15.

Over lunch, Milton offered to host and said it was his treat. He recommended the appetizers and then chose to recap the year. "If a problem comes up, I will handle it calmly now," he said -- a first conditional from his problem-solving lessons. He added that in the US you should get to the point quickly, using a modal of advice. When Karen asked, "Could you tell me what's next?", he answered with a polite indirect question of his own and then shared the plan: "We're going to expand next quarter." Finally, reporting their talk to his team, he told them she had said the timing was right. His biggest takeaway, he admitted, was how confident he had become. They raised a toast to the year ahead.

1. "If a problem comes up, I will handle it." Which structure is this?
A First conditional -- present + will (a real, likely situation).
B Third conditional -- about an impossible past.
C Reported speech.
2. Karen said: "The timing is right." How does Milton report it?
A He said the timing is right.
B He said the timing was right. (present → past)
C He said the timing will be right.
3. Which is the correct polite indirect question?
A Could you tell me what is next?
B Could you tell me what's next? (no inversion after "tell me")
C Could you tell me what next is being?

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Five Structures from Lessons 9-15

GRAMMAR

This is a review: one quick reminder of each structure you've learned, so you can move between them in one conversation.

SkillStructureExample
Problem solvingfirst conditional (present + will)If it's damaged, I will reimburse you.
Cultural advicemodals (should, ought to)You should allow time for small talk.
Polite requestsindirect questions (no inversion)Could you tell me when it ships?
Future planswill / be going toWe're going to expand next quarter.
Reporting back: say / tell + backshift -- She said the timing was right; she told me she would call. PT: esta aula nao traz gramatica nova -- ela junta tudo. Primeiro condicional para problemas (present + will), modais para conselho, perguntas indiretas educadas (sem inversao), formas de futuro (will / going to) e reported speech para relatar (volte o verbo um passo: presente vira passado, will vira would; use "tell" com a pessoa e "say" sem ela).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line with the right word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada frase.

"Please, this lunch is my ."
"I'd the appetizers -- they're the best part."
"If the order is late, I tell you the moment it ships."
Karen: "The timing is right." → She said the timing right.
"My biggest this year is confidence."

Stage 2: Put the Lunch in Order

Order

Put the moments of hosting a business lunch in a natural order. / Coloque os momentos de receber alguem em um almoco em uma ordem natural.

?Recap the year together and share what's coming next.
?Raise a toast to a great year and the ones ahead.
?Welcome the client and offer to host the meal as your treat.
?Reflect honestly on your biggest takeaway from the year.
?Recommend the appetizers and order something to start.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

Thank you for coming -- please, this lunch is my treat.
Obrigado por vir -- por favor, este almoco e por minha conta.
I'd recommend the appetizers -- they're the best part.
Eu recomendaria as entradas -- sao a melhor parte.
Could you tell me what you're planning for next season?
Voce poderia me dizer o que esta planejando para a proxima temporada?
My biggest takeaway this year is that I feel confident.
Minha maior licao este ano e que me sinto confiante.
Let's raise a toast -- to a great year and the ones ahead.
Vamos fazer um brinde -- a um otimo ano e aos que virao.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

Your client arrives for lunch. How do you host warmly?
A "You can pay for yourself."
B "Thank you for coming -- please, this lunch is my treat."
C "Why are you late?"
A piece might arrive damaged. How do you reassure the client (first conditional)?
A "If it will arrive damaged, I would reimburse you."
B "If a piece arrives damaged, I'll reimburse you right away."
C "If a piece arrived damaged, I will reimbursed you."
You want to ask politely about her plans. Which is correct?
A "Could you tell me what are you planning?"
B "Could you tell me what you're planning?"
C "Could you tell me what planning you?"
You report to your team what the client said: "I'll confirm next week." Which is right?
A "She said me she will confirm next week."
B "She told me she would confirm next week."
C "She told she will confirm next week."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute reflection on your year of English. First, recap what you can do now that you couldn't before. Then use a third conditional ("A year ago I would have..."), a future form ("Next, I'm going to..."), and one reported-speech sentence ("My teacher said..."). End by naming your biggest takeaway and how confident you feel. / Grave dois minutos refletindo sobre seu ano de ingles, usando terceiro condicional, uma forma de futuro e reported speech.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Thank you for coming -- please, this lunch is my treat.Obrigado por vir -- por favor, este almoco e por minha conta.
2I'd recommend the appetizers -- they're the best part.Eu recomendaria as entradas -- sao a melhor parte.
3Could you tell me what you're planning for next season?Voce poderia me dizer o que esta planejando para a proxima temporada?
4My biggest takeaway this year is that I feel confident.Minha maior licao este ano e que me sinto confiante.
5Let's raise a toast -- to a great year and the ones ahead.Vamos fazer um brinde -- a um otimo ano e aos que virao.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Financial Discussions -- Budgets, Forecasts & the Third Conditional

Talk money with confidence on a quarterly review call with Karen. Present a budget result, read a forecast, and explain what drives the profit -- expenses, overhead, margins and cash flow. Then learn the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + past participle) to look back at a costly decision and say what you would have done differently. New finance words: forecast, expenses, overhead, profit, allocate, surplus, projection, cash flow. PT: fale de dinheiro com seguranca em uma chamada de review -- orcamento, previsao e lucro -- e use o terceiro condicional para refletir sobre uma decisao do passado.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The precise language of a financial review. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario preciso de uma reuniao financeira.

Forecast -- previsao / projecao
a prediction of future numbers, like sales or growth, based on current data
"The forecast shows fifteen percent growth next quarter."
Expenses -- despesas / custos
the costs a business pays out, such as shipping, materials or salaries
"Shipping expenses went up again this quarter."
Overhead -- custos fixos / despesas gerais
the ongoing fixed costs of running a business, like rent, admin and utilities
"Our overhead stays the same whether we sell ten pieces or a hundred."
Profit -- lucro
the money left over after all costs and expenses are paid
"Higher shipping costs cut into our profit on every order."
Allocate -- alocar / destinar
to set aside a part of the budget for a specific purpose
"Let's allocate more to logistics to protect the margin."
Surplus -- excedente / sobra
an extra amount of money left over after all costs are covered
"The collection came in under budget, so we have a surplus to reinvest."
Projection -- projecao (de numeros futuros)
a calculated estimate of future figures, shown over time
"The projection on page two covers the next three quarters."
Cash flow -- fluxo de caixa
the movement of money into and out of a business over time
"Profit looks good, but the cash flow is tight until clients pay."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Forecast
Expenses
Overhead
Profit
Allocate
Surplus
Projection
Cash flow

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read how Milton reviews the quarter, then answer. Notice the third conditional. / Leia como o Milton revisa o trimestre e responda; observe o terceiro condicional.

On the quarterly review, Milton opened with good news: the spring collection came in under budget, leaving a healthy surplus, and the forecast pointed to fifteen percent growth. Then he flagged a concern. The overhead from shipping and customs expenses was climbing and eating into the profit on every order. Looking back, he was honest: "If we had allocated more to logistics on the winter orders, we would have kept the margin." He admitted that if he had read the early projection more carefully, he would have seen the problem sooner. His plan was simple: allocate the surplus to shipping up front and watch the cash flow until clients paid. "If we protect the margin now," he said, "next quarter's profit will hold."

1. "If we had allocated more, we would have kept the margin." What time is this about?
A The past -- an imagined result that did not happen (third conditional).
B A real plan for the future.
C Something happening right now.
2. What is the correct structure of the third conditional?
A If + present, will + verb.
B If + had + past participle, would have + past participle.
C If + would have, would have.
3. Why does Milton mention overhead?
A Because it is falling fast.
B Because it is climbing and eating into the profit on every order.
C Because it has nothing to do with profit.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Third Conditional

GRAMMAR

Use the third conditional to talk about an imagined past -- a decision that is now impossible to change.

FormStructureExample
Affirmativeif + had + participle, would have + participleIf we had allocated more, we would have kept the margin.
Negativeif + had not + participle, would not have + participleIf we hadn't lost the margin, we wouldn't have cut staff.
Questionwhat would have happened if + had + participle?What would have happened if we had read the forecast sooner?
Key contrast: first conditional = real future (If we cut overhead, we will save). Third conditional = imagined past (If we had cut overhead, we would have saved). PT: o terceiro condicional fala de um PASSADO que NAO aconteceu e do resultado imaginado. Estrutura: se + tinha/houvesse + participio (had + participle), teria + participio (would have + participle). NUNCA use "would have" na parte do "if" -- so no resultado. E a lingua de refletir sobre o que voce teria feito diferente.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line with the right word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada frase.

"The collection came in under budget, so we have a ."
"The rising is eating into our profit."
"If we more to shipping, we would have kept the margin."
"If I had read the forecast sooner, I the problem."
"Let's the surplus to logistics."

Stage 2: Put the Review in Order

Order

Put the steps of a financial review in a natural order. / Coloque as etapas de um review financeiro em uma ordem natural.

?Flag the concern: rising overhead is eating into the profit.
?Propose the plan: allocate the surplus to logistics.
?Open with the good news: under budget, with a surplus.
?Reflect on a past mistake with a third conditional.
?Read the forecast for next quarter.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

The collection came in under budget, so we have a surplus.
A colecao ficou abaixo do orcamento, entao temos um excedente.
The forecast looks strong, around fifteen percent growth.
A previsao parece forte, cerca de quinze por cento de crescimento.
The overhead is eating into our profit on every order.
Os custos fixos estao corroendo nosso lucro em cada pedido.
If we had allocated more to shipping, we would have kept the margin.
Se tivessemos alocado mais para o frete, teriamos mantido a margem.
Let's allocate the surplus to logistics to protect the margin.
Vamos alocar o excedente para a logistica para proteger a margem.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

You want to report that costs stayed below plan. Which is best?
A "We spent too much again."
B "The collection came in under budget, so we have a surplus."
C "I don't know the numbers."
You reflect on a past mistake. Which third conditional is correct?
A "If we would have allocated more, we would have kept the margin."
B "If we had allocated more, we would have kept the margin."
C "If we allocated more, we will keep the margin."
Profit looks fine but money is tight until clients pay. How do you say it?
A "The forecast is wrong."
B "Profit looks good, but the cash flow is tight until clients pay."
C "We have no profit at all."
You propose a fix for rising costs. Which is best?
A "Let's ignore the overhead."
B "Let's allocate the surplus to logistics to protect the margin."
C "Let's spend more on everything."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute review of a real financial decision from your business. First report one number (a budget result, a forecast or the profit). Then use two third-conditional sentences about what you would have done differently ("If we had..., we would have..."). Finish with one plan for next quarter ("Next, we're going to allocate..."). / Grave dois minutos sobre uma decisao financeira real, usando dois terceiros condicionais e um plano.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1The collection came in under budget, so we have a surplus.A colecao ficou abaixo do orcamento, entao temos um excedente.
2The forecast looks strong, around fifteen percent growth.A previsao parece forte, cerca de quinze por cento de crescimento.
3The overhead is eating into our profit on every order.Os custos fixos estao corroendo nosso lucro em cada pedido.
4If we had allocated more to shipping, we would have kept the margin.Se tivessemos alocado mais para o frete, teriamos mantido a margem.
5Let's allocate the surplus to logistics to protect the margin.Vamos alocar o excedente para a logistica para proteger a margem.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Pitching for Investment

Step in front of an investor and win backing for the next stage of your export business. Learn emphasis (cleft) sentences to spotlight what makes the company special -- "What sets us apart is the margin", "It is the bottleneck that holds us back" -- and the language of a confident pitch: investor, equity, stake, valuation, traction, scalable, backer, dividend. Lead with your edge, prove it with traction, handle a tough question, and state a clear ask. PT: aprenda frases de enfase e o vocabulario de investimento para apresentar seu negocio a um investidor com seguranca.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

The language of raising money from an investor. Tap Listen to hear each word. / O vocabulario de captar investimento.

Investor -- investidor(a)
a person or company that puts money into a business hoping it will grow
"She's an investor who backs premium brands."
Equity -- participacao societaria
ownership in a company, usually measured in shares or a percentage
"In exchange for the money, we give up some equity."
Stake -- fatia / parte da empresa
a share of ownership in a company that someone holds
"We're offering a fifteen percent stake."
Valuation -- avaliacao / valor da empresa
the total amount a company is considered to be worth
"We're raising at a valuation of ten million."
Traction -- tracao / prova de crescimento
real evidence that a business is growing, such as sales or repeat customers
"Our repeat clients are the clearest traction we have."
Scalable -- escalavel
able to grow much larger without a big rise in cost or loss of quality
"With more capacity, the model is fully scalable."
Backer -- apoiador(a) / financiador(a)
a person or fund that supports a business, usually with money
"The right backer brings contacts, not just cash."
Dividend -- dividendo
a share of a company's profit paid out to its owners or shareholders
"Early on, we'd reinvest profit rather than pay a dividend."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition. / Combine cada palavra com sua definicao.

Investor
Equity
Stake
Valuation
Traction
Scalable
Backer
Dividend

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read how Milton frames his pitch, then answer. Notice the emphasis (cleft) sentences. / Leia como o Milton estrutura o pitch e responda; observe as frases de enfase.

In front of the investor, Milton kept it sharp. "What sets us apart is the margin," he began. "It is our craftsmanship and our story that let us price above the market and still sell out." For proof, he pointed to the traction: revenue up forty percent and more than half of buyers reordering. When she doubted whether a handmade brand was scalable, he answered calmly: "It is the bottleneck, not demand, that holds us back." Then came the ask: one and a half million for a fifteen percent stake, at a valuation of ten million. "For the right backer, the return is clear," he closed. He explained that early on they would reinvest profit rather than pay a dividend, giving up a little equity now to grow far bigger later.

1. "What sets us apart is the margin." What is this structure doing?
A An emphasis (cleft) sentence -- it spotlights one idea (the margin).
B A reported-speech sentence.
C A first conditional.
2. In "It is the bottleneck that holds us back," which connector is correct?
A It is the bottleneck which holds us back.
B It is the bottleneck that holds us back. (use "that" in this cleft)
C It is the bottleneck what holds us back.
3. What exactly is Milton offering the investor?
A A dividend of fifteen percent.
B A fifteen percent stake for one and a half million, at a ten million valuation.
C All of the equity in the company.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Emphasis (Cleft) Sentences

GRAMMAR

Split one plain sentence into two parts to push one idea into the spotlight. Use them at the moments that matter most.

PatternFrameExample
What-cleftWhat + clause + is/are + focusWhat sets us apart is the margin.
It-cleftIt is + focus + that + clauseIt is the bottleneck that holds us back.
What we needWhat we need is + focusWhat we need is capacity.
Common mistake: never drop the linking verb (What sets us apart is the margin, not "What sets us apart the margin"); the verb agrees with the focus (What we need is capacity); and in "It is... ", the connector is that, not which. PT: frases de enfase dividem uma frase comum em duas para destacar UMA ideia. Use "What + oracao + is/are + foco" ou "It is + foco + that + oracao". Nunca esqueca o verbo de ligacao (is/are), faca o verbo concordar com o foco, e use "that" (nao "which") na forma "It is...". Use com moderacao, nos momentos-chave: seu diferencial, sua prova, seu pedido.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line with the right word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. / Complete cada frase.

"What sets us apart the margin."
"It is the bottleneck holds us back."
"We're offering a fifteen percent ."
"Our repeat clients are the real here."
"For the right , the return is clear."

Stage 2: Put the Pitch in Order

Order

Put the moments of an investor pitch in a natural order. / Coloque os momentos de um pitch em uma ordem natural.

?Answer the tough question about whether it's scalable.
?Frame the return for the backer and invite them into the numbers.
?Lead with your edge using an emphasis sentence.
?State the ask: the amount, the stake and the valuation.
?Show the traction: growth and repeat clients.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. / Ouca cada frase e grave-se.

What sets us apart is the margin.
O que nos diferencia e a margem.
Our repeat clients are the real traction here.
Nossos clientes recorrentes sao a verdadeira prova de crescimento.
We're raising one and a half million for a fifteen percent stake.
Estamos captando um milhao e meio por uma fatia de quinze por cento.
With more capacity, the model is fully scalable.
Com mais capacidade, o modelo e totalmente escalavel.
For the right backer, the return is clear.
Para o apoiador certo, o retorno e claro.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation. / Escolha a melhor resposta.

You open the pitch and want to spotlight your edge. Which is best?
A "We have a good margin and some other things too."
B "What sets us apart is the margin."
C "The margin, it's there."
The investor doubts the business can grow. How do you answer?
A "It will never really grow much."
B "With more capacity, the model is fully scalable."
C "We don't think about growth."
You want to state your ask clearly. Which is correct?
A "We are raising for a fifteen percent stake one million."
B "We're raising one and a half million for a fifteen percent stake."
C "We want a stake of money fifteen."
You want to spotlight the real cause of the limit. Which emphasis sentence is right?
A "It is the bottleneck which holds us back."
B "It is the bottleneck that holds us back."
C "The bottleneck holds us back it is."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt. There is no right or wrong answer. / Grave-se respondendo. Nao ha resposta certa ou errada.

Record a two-minute investor pitch for your own business. Lead with an emphasis sentence ("What sets us apart is..."), present one number as proof of your traction, name your ask with a stake and a valuation, and close by framing the return for a backer. / Grave um pitch de dois minutos: comece com uma frase de enfase, mostre um numero como prova de traction, faca o ask com stake e valuation, e feche enquadrando o retorno para o backer.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1What sets us apart is the margin.O que nos diferencia e a margem.
2Our repeat clients are the real traction here.Nossos clientes recorrentes sao a verdadeira prova de crescimento.
3With more capacity, the model is fully scalable.Com mais capacidade, o modelo e totalmente escalavel.
4We're raising one and a half million for a fifteen percent stake.Estamos captando um milhao e meio por uma fatia de quinze por cento.
5For the right backer, the return is clear.Para o apoiador certo, o retorno e claro.
01
Diagnostic and Reactivation
Who Is Milton Sayegh in English? — 38 slides
02
The Language of Your Business
Products and Operations — 39 slides
03
Making Connections
Networking at Trade Shows — 39 slides
04
Negotiation Essentials
Setting Terms and Closing Deals — 39 slides
05
Board Meetings
Participating and Expressing Opinions — 39 slides
06
Email Etiquette
Writing Clear Business Emails — 32 slides
07
Numbers and Data
Presenting Financial Information — 32 slides
08
Review and Consolidation
Putting Lessons 1-7 Together — 32 slides
09
Contracts and Agreements
Legal Vocabulary & the Past Simple — 32 slides
10
Supply Chain
From Brazil to the U.S. - the Passive Voice — 32 slides
11
Problem Solving
When Things Go Wrong - First & Second Conditionals — 32 slides
12
Cultural Intelligence — Brazilian vs American Business
Read the room across cultures: punctuality, directness, small talk and hierarchy, and advise with modals (should, ought to, had better, be supposed to) — 32 slides
13
Closing the Deal — Negotiating Price & Terms
Negotiate price and terms like a pro: counteroffers, concessions, compromise and closing — and ask for what you want with polite, indirect questions (Could you..., Would you mind..., I was wondering if...) — 32 slides
14
After the Deal — Delivery & Keeping the Client
Run everything after the handshake: confirm the invoice, track the shipment, hit each milestone, handle after-sales and onboard a client to keep — and talk about what WILL happen and what you ARE going to do with future forms (will, be going to, present continuous for arrangements) — 32 slides
15
Handling Problems After Delivery — Reporting & Saving the Relationship
When something goes wrong after the goods arrive, the relationship is on the line. Acknowledge the issue, reassure the client, escalate it to the right person, sort out a discrepancy, and protect the goodwill you built — then relay it all to your team with reported speech (she said the box was damaged, he told me he would reimburse us) — 32 slides
16
Review & Consolidation — The Business Lunch
A checkpoint over a celebratory business lunch with Karen: recap the whole journey from Lessons 9-15 and put it all together — small talk, problem solving (conditionals), cultural intelligence (modals), polite indirect questions, future plans (will / going to) and reporting back (reported speech) — while you host a meal, recommend a dish, raise a toast, and reflect out loud on how far your English has come — 32 slides
17
Financial Discussions — Budgets, Forecasts & the Third Conditional
Talk money with confidence on a quarterly review call with Karen: present a budget, read a forecast, and explain what drives the profit on a collection — expenses, overhead, margins and cash flow — then look back at a costly mistake and discuss what you would have done differently using the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + participle), the natural language of reviewing financial decisions — 31 slides
18
Pitching for Investment
Milton steps in front of an investor to win backing for the next stage of the export business. Learn to frame what makes the company special with emphasis (cleft) sentences, present the numbers, handle a sharp question and ask for a clear commitment — equity, a stake, traction, a backer, a valuation, returns — and close a confident pitch that makes an investor lean in — 31 slides

Complementares — Aula 1

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
Succession — Season 1, Episode 1

Dinâmicas de poder corporativo, inglês formal em board meetings e negociações familiares. Perfeito para o contexto de Milton.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção no vocabulário de negócios e nas expressões formais.

Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) — "David Yurman: David Yurman Jewelry"

A história de como David Yurman construiu seu império de joias. Conexão direta com o universo de Milton.

Dica: ouça no carro ou durante exercícios. Anote 3 palavras novas que ouvir.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Business English Pod — "BEP 301: Business Introductions"

Vídeo curto sobre como se apresentar profissionalmente em inglês. Prático e direto ao ponto.

Dica: repita as frases em voz alta junto com o vídeo. Pratique a entonação.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 2

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o vocabulário de negócios e operações.

Série
Billions (Showtime) — Season 1, Episode 1

Negociações de alto risco, vocabulário de negócios avançado, dinâmicas de poder. Excelente para absorver linguagem corporativa.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção nas palavras: deal, revenue, market share, competitor.

Podcast
The Tim Ferriss Show — "Tim Ferriss interviews a luxury goods CEO"

Entrevista sobre estratégia de negócios no segmento de luxo. Vocabulário direto sobre operações e crescimento.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 20 minutos. Anote como o CEO descreve produtos e operações.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy — "Business English Vocabulary"

Vídeo curto com vocabulário essencial de negócios: supply chain, revenue, wholesale, retail.

Dica: pause e repita cada termo em voz alta. Tente usar 3 palavras novas em uma frase sobre sua empresa.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 3

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar vocabulário de networking e trade shows.

Série
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1

Networking profissional, negociações de alto nível e como causar uma boa primeira impressão. Excelente para absorver linguagem de networking.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção em como os personagens se apresentam e trocam cartões.

Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) — "Kendra Scott: Kendra Scott Jewelry"

A história de como Kendra Scott construiu sua marca de joias por meio de networking e feiras. Conexão direta com o tema da aula.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 20 minutos. Anote como ela descreve suas experiências em trade shows.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Greg — "Networking in English"

Vídeo prático com frases essenciais para networking em inglês: como se apresentar, trocar cartões e agendar follow-ups.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase em voz alta. Pratique se apresentar como Milton faria em uma feira.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 4

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar vocabulário de negociação e modal verbs.

Filme
The Pursuit of Happyness (2006)

Negociação sob pressão, cultura de negócios americana e resiliência. Will Smith interpreta um vendedor que precisa negociar para sobreviver.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção nas cenas de negociação e nas expressões com could, would, should.

Podcast
The Tim Ferriss Show — "Negotiation Masterclass"

Estratégias de negociação usadas por executivos de alto nível. Vocabulário direto sobre termos, concessões e acordos.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 20 minutos. Anote frases com could, would e should usadas pelos entrevistados.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Business English Pod — "BEP 302: Negotiating a Deal"

Vídeo prático com frases essenciais para negociação em inglês: como propor termos, fazer concessões e fechar acordos.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase em voz alta. Pratique as expressões com could e would.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 5

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
Succession — Season 1, Episode 2

Dinâmicas de conselho, poder corporativo e linguagem formal de reuniões. Conecta diretamente com o vocabulário de board meetings da Aula 5.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção nas expressões formais usadas em reuniões de conselho.

Podcast
Business English Pod — "BEP 320: Participating in Meetings"

Frases essenciais para participar de reuniões em inglês: como fazer propostas, expressar opiniões e votar.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 15 minutos. Anote frases com "I would like to," "I move to," e "Have we discussed."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Rachel's English — "How to Sound Confident in Meetings"

Pronúncia e entrega para soar confiante em reuniões formais em inglês. Foco em entonação e clareza.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase em voz alta. Pratique a entonação ao fazer propostas e expressar opiniões.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 6

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
Suits — Season 1, Episode 4: "Dirty Little Secrets"

Mike redige um e-mail urgente para um cliente com deadline apertado. Preste atenção em como ele usa "subject line," "CC," "attachment" e "follow up" em contexto corporativo real. Conecta diretamente com o vocabulário de email writing da Aula 6.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote as expressões de abertura e fechamento dos e-mails que aparecem na tela.

Podcast
Business English Pod — "BEP 173: Writing Effective Emails"

Estrutura de e-mails profissionais: greeting, body, closing e follow-up. Ensina como usar "Dear," "Best regards" e "Please find attached" em contextos reais de negócios internacionais.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 10 minutos. Anote as expressões de abertura e fechamento que Milton usa na Aula 6: "Dear Mr. Thompson," "Best regards."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy — "How to Write a Professional Email" (12 min)

Guia completo de e-mails profissionais: subject line, greeting, body structure e closing. Lucy mostra exemplos reais de "I am writing to inform you," "Please do not hesitate to contact me" e "I look forward to hearing from you."

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e tente escrever um e-mail para um cliente imaginário da Sayegh Jewelry usando as mesmas estruturas.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 7

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
Billions — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Bobby Axelrod apresenta resultados financeiros trimestrais aos investidores usando "profit," "growth," "forecast" e "bottom line." Preste atenção em como ele compara períodos usando comparativos: "higher than last quarter," "the most profitable market."

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 5 termos financeiros e compare com o vocabulário da Aula 7: quarter, profit, growth, turnover.

Podcast
Business English Pod — "BEP 326: Discussing Financial Results"

Como apresentar resultados financeiros em inglês: "Our profit was higher than expected," "The forecast for next quarter is positive," "Turnover grew by 18%." Conecta diretamente com o vocabulário de financial reports da Aula 7.

Dica: ouça prestando atenção nos comparativos (higher, lower, more profitable). Pratique descrevendo os resultados da Sayegh Jewelry.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Harvard Business Review — "How to Present Financial Data Effectively" (8 min)

Técnicas visuais e verbais para apresentar dados financeiros: como descrever gráficos ("As you can see in the chart"), comparar períodos ("compared to last year") e destacar tendências ("growth of 18%").

Dica: pause e repita as frases de transição: "Let me walk you through the figures," "This chart shows," "The bottom line is."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 8

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
Mad Men — Season 1, Episode 1: "Smoke Gets in Your Eyes"

Don Draper recebe um cliente no escritório e conduz uma reunião de apresentação. Usa "agenda," "schedule," "client" e "deal" enquanto apresenta uma proposta de campanha. Conecta com o vocabulário de client visits da Aula 8.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção nas expressões de hospitalidade: "Thank you for coming," "Let me walk you through our process."

Podcast
Business English Pod — "BEP 305: Hosting a Client Visit"

Como receber um cliente internacional: cumprimentos, apresentação da empresa, tour pelo escritório. Inclui "Could we schedule a meeting," "How long have you worked with this supplier" e expressões com Present Perfect e Present Continuous.

Dica: ouça e anote as perguntas que o host faz ao cliente. Compare com as frases do diálogo da Aula 8: "How long have you worked with this supplier?"

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Speak Confident English — "Business English: Hosting Clients at Your Office" (10 min)

Guia prático para receber clientes: small talk, tour pela empresa, oferecer bebidas e transição para a reunião. Frases como "We are launching a new collection," "We have worked together for many years."

Dica: pratique o roteiro imaginando que está recebendo um buyer americano na Sayegh Jewelry. Use as frases: "Let me show you our showroom."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 9

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
Suits — Season 2, Episode 1: "She Knows"

Harvey Specter revisa um contrato de distribuição cláusula por cláusula com o cliente. Usa "terms," "clause," "breach," "agreement" e "signature" em negociação real. Conecta diretamente com o vocabulário de contratos da Aula 9.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote as frases que Harvey usa para discutir termos: "Both parties must respect the agreement."

Podcast
Business English Pod — "BEP 284: Negotiating a Contract"

Negociação de termos contratuais em inglês: "We agreed on the main conditions," "The distributor accepted our terms," "We signed the contract last week." Usa Past Simple em contexto jurídico-comercial.

Dica: ouça focando nos verbos no Past Simple: agreed, signed, sent, reviewed. Compare com as frases da Aula 9.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Greg — "Legal English: Contract Vocabulary Explained" (9 min)

Explicação clara de termos contratuais essenciais: agreement, clause, breach, obligation, party e signature. Cada termo é explicado com exemplos reais de contratos internacionais.

Dica: pause após cada termo e escreva uma frase usando-o no contexto da Sayegh Jewelry: "We signed a new agreement with our US distributor."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementares — Aula 10

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Série
The Crown — Season 1, Episode 3: "Windsor"

Cena de logística real: transporte de objetos preciosos entre palácios com escoltas, alfândega e verificação. Usa "cargo," "shipment," "customs" e "warehouse" em contexto de transporte de alto valor, conectando com o vocabulário de supply chain da Aula 10.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção em como descrevem o processo de transporte: "The cargo was checked," "The shipment was delivered."

Podcast
Business English Pod — "BEP 341: Supply Chain and Logistics Vocabulary"

Vocabulário essencial de logística internacional: "The containers are sent by sea," "The order was shipped last Friday," "Everything was checked at customs." Usa Passive Voice em contexto real de exportação.

Dica: ouça focando nas frases na voz passiva (was shipped, was checked, are transported). Descreva o processo da Sayegh Jewelry usando essas estruturas.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Wendover Productions — "How Container Ships Changed the World" (11 min)

Documentário sobre como o transporte marítimo revolucionou o comércio global: containers, freight, customs, supply chain. Mostra o processo completo de exportação, do warehouse ao porto de destino.

Dica: anote 5 termos de logística que você reconhece da Aula 10. Depois descreva o processo da Sayegh: "Our products are made in Brazil and exported to the United States."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 -- Problem Solving: When Things Go Wrong

Series
The Bear (FX/Hulu) -- a kitchen-crisis episode (e.g. S1E7 "Review")

A high-pressure business where things go wrong constantly. Watch how the staff name a problem fast, stay calm, and commit to a fix instead of making excuses — the same control you need when a luxury client calls with a complaint.

Tip: each time something breaks, write the first conditional the team uses or could use: "If we ___, we will ___."

Podcast
The Customer Service Revolution (John DiJulius) -- any episode on service recovery

Real cases of how strong businesses turn a complaint into loyalty: apologize once, own the problem, and resolve it with a clear offer of replacement or compensation. The exact moves from this class, in English, from real companies.

Tip: note one phrase the host uses to apologize with control, and one offer of compensation. Reuse them with a real client.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "First and second conditionals" explained

A short, clear lesson on exactly the grammar from this class: if + present + will for real situations, and if + past + would for hypothetical, softer ones. Hear native speakers use both forms in natural business contexts.

Tip: after watching, say five conditionals out loud about your own export work — three first, two second.

Lesson 12 -- Cultural Intelligence: Brazilian vs American Business

Series
Suits (USA Network) -- any boardroom negotiation scene

A masterclass in American business directness: short small talk, then straight to the point, with people stating their value assertively and without apology. Watch how courtesy and bluntness coexist — exactly the balance you need with a New York buyer.

Tip: pause on one direct line and rewrite it as advice: "You should...", "You're supposed to...", "You'd better...".

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast (Harvard Business Review) -- any episode on cross-cultural business or Erin Meyer's "The Culture Map"

Practical, research-based talk on how trust, directness, and time work differently across cultures — including Brazil and the U.S. The exact framework behind this class: when directness is courtesy, and how relationship-first and results-first cultures meet in the middle.

Tip: note one difference between your culture and an American one, then say the advice aloud with a modal.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "should, ought to and had better" explained

A short, clear lesson on exactly the grammar from this class: the difference between giving advice (should / ought to) and warning someone (had better), with the base verb and no extra "to". Native speakers model it in natural contexts.

Tip: after watching, give five pieces of advice out loud about your own export work — mix should, ought to, supposed to, and had better.

Lesson 13 -- Closing the Deal: Negotiating Price & Terms

Series / Film
Shark Tank (ABC) -- any pitch with a back-and-forth on price or equity

A live masterclass in negotiation: counteroffers, concessions, leverage and the moment two sides either compromise or hit a deadlock. Watch how the investors and founders soften their asks and trade one thing for another to settle a deal.

Tip: pause on one offer and rephrase it as a polite indirect question: "Could you tell me...?", "Would you be open to...?".

Podcast
Negotiate Anything (Kwame Christian) -- any episode on price or "asking with confidence"

Practical, conversational advice on how to make a counteroffer, give a concession without losing leverage, and break a deadlock — exactly the toolkit from this class, modeled in real business language.

Tip: write down one negotiation phrase you hear, then say it back as a softened, indirect question.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Indirect questions" / polite question forms

A short, clear lesson on exactly the grammar from this class: how to soften a question with "Could you tell me...", "Do you know...", and "I was wondering if...", keeping statement order and dropping "do/does/did". Native speakers model it in natural contexts.

Tip: after watching, turn five direct questions about your own deals into polite, indirect ones out loud.

Lesson 14 -- After the Deal: Delivery & Keeping the Client
