Series / Film
The Founder (2016) -- the build-out and franchising of McDonald's

A film about what happens AFTER the deal: delivery, systems, onboarding new owners, and keeping a brand promise at scale. Watch how every milestone, every shipment, and every bit of after-sales either retains a partner or loses them.

Tip: each time a plan is announced, decide which future form fits: "will", "be going to", or present continuous.

Podcast
Business English Pod -- any episode on "delivery", "logistics" or "customer service"

Practical business English for exactly this stage: confirming an invoice, tracking a shipment, talking lead times and after-sales. The host models the future forms naturally while planning next steps with a client.

Tip: note one sentence that plans a future action, then say it back, swapping in "will", "be going to", or present continuous.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "will, going to and present continuous for the future"

A short, clear lesson on exactly the grammar from this class: when to use "will" for a decision made now, "be going to" for an existing plan, and the present continuous for a fixed arrangement. Native speakers model the difference in real situations.

Tip: after watching, describe your next client handover in five future sentences, using each form at least once.

Lesson 15 -- Handling Problems After Delivery

Series / Film
The Crown -- any episode where an advisor reports a problem to the Queen

A masterclass in handling bad news with composure: advisors acknowledge a problem, reassure, and relay exactly what someone said. Listen for the reported speech ("He said that...", "They told her...") as crises are escalated and resolved.

Tip: each time a character relays what someone said, pause and notice the backshift -- present to past, will to would.

Podcast
Business English Pod -- any episode on "complaints", "customer service" or "problem solving"

Practical business English for exactly this stage: acknowledging an issue, reassuring a client, escalating it, and offering a fix. The host models the phrases of apology and resolution, and reports back what the customer said.

Tip: note one line where a complaint is relayed, then say it back in reported speech ("She told me that...").

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Reported speech: say and tell"

A short, clear lesson on exactly the grammar from this class: how the verb shifts back in time when you report what someone said, and the difference between say and tell. Native speakers model it in real conversations.

Tip: after watching, report a recent message from a colleague in five sentences, backshifting each verb and using both say and tell.

Lesson 16 -- Review & Consolidation: The Business Lunch

Series / Film
Succession -- any business-dinner scene

Watch how executives host, recommend, recap and reflect over a meal -- and how they slip between problem talk, plans and reported conversations in a single dinner. A live model of everything from Lessons 9-15 around one table.

Tip: each time someone hosts or recaps, pause and name the structure they use (conditional, modal, future form, reported speech).

Podcast
Business English Pod -- any episode on "socializing", "networking" or "business meals"

Practical English for the relaxed side of business: hosting a guest, recommending dishes, making small talk and raising a toast. The host models the warm phrases that turn a transaction into a relationship.

Tip: note one phrase you'd use to host a client, then say it out loud as if Karen were across the table.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "English at a business lunch / making conversation"

A short, clear lesson on the social English of a business meal: welcoming a guest, recommending food, keeping a friendly conversation going, and closing warmly. Native speakers model it in real situations.

Tip: after watching, record your own one-minute "host a client" speech using host, treat, recommend, recap and toast.

Lesson 17 -- Financial Discussions: Budgets, Forecasts & the Third Conditional

Series / Film
Shark Tank -- any pitch and Q&A segment

Watch founders present budgets, forecasts, margins and cash flow under pressure -- and hear investors ask exactly the financial questions Karen would. A live model of the vocabulary from this lesson.

Tip: each time a number comes up, name it out loud -- is it the forecast, the overhead, the margin or the profit?

Podcast
Business English Pod -- any episode on "finance", "budgets" or "financial reviews"

Practical English for talking money: presenting a budget, reading a forecast, explaining cash flow and discussing profit margins. The host models the exact phrases you'd use on a quarterly review call.

Tip: note one phrase for flagging a concern, then say it out loud as if Karen were on the call.

YouTube
BBC Learning English / English with Lucy -- "the third conditional"

A short, clear lesson on the third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle. Native speakers model how to talk about an imagined past -- exactly what you need to reflect on a financial decision.

Tip: after watching, record three third-conditional sentences about real decisions in your business this year.

Lesson 18 -- Pitching for Investment

Series / Film
Shark Tank (US) -- any pitch episode

Watch founders pitch real investors: how they lead with their edge, present traction and numbers, handle sharp questions, and state a stake and a valuation. A live model of everything in this lesson.

Tip: each time a founder is challenged, pause and try to give a calm, one-line answer yourself before they do.

Podcast
The Twenty Minute VC -- any founder fundraising episode

Investors and founders talk through how real raises happen: what makes an investor lean in, how a stake and a valuation are set, and what counts as genuine traction. The vocabulary of this lesson, used by people who do it daily.

Tip: note one phrase a founder uses to frame their edge, then rewrite it as an emphasis sentence ("What sets us apart is...").

YouTube
Y Combinator -- "How to Pitch Your Startup" / "How to Talk to Investors"

A clear, practical talk on structuring an investor pitch: the story, the proof, and the ask. Native speakers model how to be direct, handle hard questions, and frame the return for a backer.

Tip: after watching, record your own one-minute pitch using stake, valuation, traction and one emphasis sentence.

The Dream
Packing Words
The Code
Getting There
Practice
Your Turn
Wrap-up
T
Lesson 1 — Milton's Return to English

Who Is Milton Sayegh
in English?

B1+ — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Let's Begin

"How was your week? Tell me about it."
"Tell me about your company in English."
"What is the hardest part of doing business in English?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Your Motivation

"Why is English important for your business?"
"When was the last time you needed English and struggled?"
"What would change if you could speak fluently?"
"A gente se faz entender lá. Mas eu gostaria de me expressar um pouco melhor."
— Milton Sayegh
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Your Business Vocabulary

10 essential words for today

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To send goods to another country for sale
Export
To send goods to another country for sale
"My company exports luxury jewelry."
Expensive, high-quality products
Luxury Goods
Expensive, high-quality products
"We specialize in luxury goods."
Rings, necklaces, bracelets — decorative items
Jewelry
Decorative items like rings, necklaces, bracelets
"The jewelry industry requires precision."
A formal meeting of company directors
Board Meeting
A formal meeting of company directors
"I attend the board meeting every month."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A discussion to reach a business agreement
Negotiation
A discussion to reach a business agreement
"A good negotiation requires clarity."
A business agreement or transaction
Deal
A business agreement or transaction
"We closed a major deal last quarter."
The main office of a company
Headquarters
The main office of a company
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo."
In or to a foreign country across the sea
Overseas
In or to a foreign country across the sea
"I travel overseas several times a year."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 9-10

A failure to understand something correctly
Misunderstanding
A failure to understand something correctly
"I want to avoid any misunderstanding."
The ability to speak a language smoothly
Fluency
The ability to speak a language smoothly
"My goal is to recover my fluency."
0 / 2 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Say It All

Export
Luxury Goods
Jewelry
Board Meeting
Negotiation
Deal
Headquarters
Overseas
Misunderstanding
Fluency
Chapter 3: The Code

Cracking
The Code

Present Simple and Present Continuous

Grammar Discovery

What Do These Have in Common?

"I export jewelry."
"We specialize in luxury goods."
"Milton runs a company."
"The company operates overseas."
Present SimpleUse
I/You/We/They + verbFacts, routines, permanent states
He/She/It + verbsAdd -s for third person
Grammar Discovery

And These?

"I am working on my English."
"We are expanding operations."
"Milton is recovering his fluency."
Present ContinuousUse
I am + verb-ingCurrent, temporary actions
He/She is + verb-ingActions in progress NOW
We/They are + verb-ingChanging situations
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I work here since 2000."
"I have worked here since 2000."

Preview: Present Perfect comes in Lesson 5

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

"My company ___exports jewelry to the U.S." (fact)
"Right now, I ___am working on my English." (temporary)
"We ___specialize in luxury goods." (permanent fact)
"The company ___is expanding operations." (current trend)
"Milton ___travels overseas every year." (routine)
Chapter 4: Getting There

A Meeting at the
Trade Show

Milton meets James Whitfield

Dialogue

At the Trade Show (1/3)

Dialogue

At the Trade Show (2/3)

Dialogue

At the Trade Show (3/3)

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

Where is James Whitfield from?
New York — he works at Whitfield Gems.
What does Milton's company specialize in?
Luxury goods — precious stones, gold pieces, and custom designs.
Where is Milton's American office?
In Miami.
Your Business Card

Milton's Card

Milton Sayegh
CEO & Export Director
Sayegh Luxury Exports
São Paulo, Brazil | Miami, FL
+55 11 9999-0000 | milton@sayeghexports.com
SAYEGH LUXURY EXPORTS
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Listening, speaking, error correction

Listening

Listen First

A news segment about Brazilian luxury exports

0:000:00
Speed
Listening Comprehension

What Did You Hear?

What sector is growing in Brazilian exports?
The luxury jewelry sector — precious stones and gold.
What is the main destination?
The United States market.
What do exporters need to succeed?
Clear communication and strong business relationships.
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
You meet someone at a conference. Introduce yourself and your company.
"Good morning. My name is Milton Sayegh. I run a luxury jewelry export company based in São Paulo."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
Someone asks about your office locations. Answer.
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo, but we also have an office in Miami."
1 / 6
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"I have 30 years of business."
"I have been in business for 30 years." (or "I have 30 years of experience.")
"Our headquarter is in São Paulo."
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo." (always ends in -s)
"I travel to overseas every year."
"I travel overseas every year." (no preposition needed)
"We are specialize in luxury goods."
"We specialize in luxury goods." (fact = Present Simple, no 'are')
"I work here since 2000."
"I have worked here since 2000." (Present Perfect with 'since')
0 / 5
Oral Drilling

Say It in English

Say your name and what you do for a living.
"My name is Milton Sayegh. I am in the jewelry export business."
Say where your company is based and where it operates.
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo, but we operate in the United States."
Say how often you travel abroad for business.
"I travel overseas several times a year."
Say what you are currently doing to improve your English.
"I am working on improving my English for negotiations."
Ask someone to repeat something you did not understand.
"Could you say that again, please?"
Say what your goal is with English.
"My goal is to recover my fluency in business English."
Sentence Building

Build the Sentence

jewelrycompanyexportsluxuryMy
"My company exports luxury jewelry."
overseastraveltimesIseverala year
"I travel overseas several times a year."
am workingonEnglishimprovingImy
"I am working on improving my English."
negotiationrequiresgoodAclarity
"A good negotiation requires clarity."
headquartesisSão PauloinOur
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Show What
You've Got

From guided to free production

Guided Role-play

Introduce Yourself at a Trade Show

Guided: Use the keywords

You are at a jewelry trade show in Las Vegas. A potential partner approaches you. Introduce yourself and your company using the keywords below.
namejewelryexportSão Pauloluxury goodsheadquartersoverseasthirty years
Semi-free Role-play

Describe Your Company

Semi-free: Complete the sentences

Tell your new partner about your company. Start each sentence and finish it yourself:
"My company ..."
"We specialize in ..."
"Our headquarters ..."
"I travel ..."
"Right now, I am working on ..."
"My goal is ..."
Free Role-play

First Meeting with a New Distributor

Free: No support — just talk!

You meet a new American distributor for the first time. Introduce yourself, describe your company, and express interest in working together. No keywords, no script — just you.
Delayed Error Correction

Let's Fine-tune

Errors noted during free production

Write Milton's errors here during the live session.
Use the Zoom chat or whiteboard.
Chapter 7: Wrap-up

Wrapping
Up

Survival Card

5 Phrases to Survive

1
My name is Milton Sayegh. I am in the jewelry business.
2
I am responsible for all export operations.
3
Our headquarters is in São Paulo, but we operate in the U.S.
4
I travel overseas several times a year.
5
Could you say that again, please?
What I Learned

Lesson 1 Checklist

I can introduce myself and my company in English.
I know 10 business vocabulary words.
I understand the difference between Present Simple and Present Continuous.
I had a conversation at a trade show in English.
I can describe my company without help.
I can ask someone to repeat something politely.
I know my 5 Survival Card phrases.
I identified and corrected common mistakes.
I completed a free role-play without support.
I feel ready for Lesson 2.

Stamp Earned!

Day 1: Who Is Milton Sayegh in English? — Complete

Lesson 2: The Language of Your Business

Products and Operations — Company Profile Presentation

Day 1 — Complete

Lesson 2 — The Language of Your Business

Products and
Operations

B1+ — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review — Lesson 1

"What does your company export?"
"Where are your headquarters?"
"Tell me about a recent deal or negotiation."
Chapter 1: The Dream

Your Business

"Do you sell directly to consumers or through other companies?"
"Who are your main competitors in the luxury segment?"
"How does your product get from Brazil to the U.S.?"
Key Expressions

Business Collocations

run a business
to manage and operate a company
expand operations
to grow your business into new areas
close a deal
to finalize a business agreement
Chapter 2: Packing Words

The Language of
Business

10 essential words for products and operations

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To focus on a particular area of work
Specialize
To focus on a particular area of work
"We specialize in premium gemstones."
Selling directly to consumers
Retail
Selling directly to consumers
"Some pieces are in retail stores."
Selling in large quantities to businesses
Wholesale
Selling in large quantities to businesses
"We sell wholesale to retailers."
A company that supplies goods to stores
Distributor
A company that supplies goods to stores
"A reliable distributor is essential."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

The process from production to delivery
Supply Chain
The process from production to delivery
"Our supply chain covers sourcing to delivery."
Income from business activities
Revenue
Income from business activities
"Our revenue has grown steadily."
Percentage of total sales in a market
Market Share
Percentage of total sales in a market
"Our market share is growing."
A customer of a professional service
Client
A customer, especially of a professional service
"Our clients value quality."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 9-10

A rival company in the same market
Competitor
A rival company
"Our competitors do not offer the same quality."
A business relationship between companies
Partnership
A business relationship between companies
"I would like to discuss a partnership."
0 / 2 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Say It All

Specialize
Retail
Wholesale
Distributor
Supply Chain
Revenue
Market Share
Client
Competitor
Partnership
Chapter 3: The Code

Present Simple
Deep Dive

Affirmative, Negative, Interrogative

Grammar Discovery

Affirmative: What Do These Have in Common?

"Milton exports jewelry."
"His company specializes in luxury goods."
"We work with three distributors."
"Our revenue comes from wholesale."
SubjectVerb
I / You / We / Theyexport, work, specialize
He / She / Itexports, works, specializes
Grammar Discovery

Negative: What Changes?

"He does not sell directly to consumers."
"His competitors do not offer the same quality."
"We do not have direct retail partnerships."
"The company does not have many competitors."
SubjectNegative
I / You / We / Theydo not + verb (base form)
He / She / Itdoes not + verb (base form, NO -s!)
Grammar Discovery

Interrogative: How to Ask?

"Does Milton work with American clients?"
"How many distributors does he have?"
"Do you handle the supply chain yourselves?"
"Does your company sell retail or wholesale?"
SubjectQuestion
I / You / We / TheyDo + subject + verb (base)?
He / She / ItDoes + subject + verb (base)?
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Does he exports jewelry?"
"Does he export jewelry?"
"He does not sells directly."
"He does not sell directly."
Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

"Our company ___specializes in premium gemstones." (3rd person)
"He ___does not sell directly to consumers." (negative)
"___Does Milton work with American clients?" (question)
"We ___do not have direct retail partnerships." (negative)
"Our revenue ___comes from wholesale." (3rd person)
"How many distributors ___does he have?" (question)
Chapter 4: Getting There

Meeting a
Retail Partner

Milton meets Sarah Chen from California

Dialogue

The Meeting (1/3)

Dialogue

The Meeting (2/3)

Dialogue

The Meeting (3/3)

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

How did Sarah hear about Milton's company?
A distributor in New York recommended him.
Does Milton have direct retail partnerships?
Not yet — he currently works through distributors only.
Who handles the supply chain?
Milton's company manages everything from sourcing to delivery.
Company Profile

Sayegh Luxury Exports

Company Profile
Sayegh Luxury Exports
Specialization: Premium Brazilian gemstones & custom gold
Sales model: Wholesale & distributor network
Markets: United States (3 major distributors)
Supply chain: Fully managed, sourcing to delivery
Competitors: Few in the premium segment
SAYEGH LUXURY EXPORTS — SÃO PAULO | MIAMI
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Listening, speaking, error correction

Listening

Listen First

A jewelry company describes its products and operations

0:000:00
Speed
Listening Comprehension

What Did You Hear?

What does the company specialize in?
Premium gemstones and custom gold designs.
How does the company sell its products?
Through wholesale partners and distributors.
What is the company looking for?
New retail partnerships to expand their market share.
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
Describe what your company specializes in.
"We specialize in premium Brazilian gemstones and custom gold designs."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
Say that you do NOT sell directly to consumers.
"We do not sell directly to consumers. We work through distributors."
1 / 6
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"He does not sells retail."
"He does not sell retail." (base form after does not)
"Does your company exports jewelry?"
"Does your company export jewelry?" (base form after does)
"Our company specialize in gemstones."
"Our company specializes in gemstones." (3rd person -s)
"We not have many competitors."
"We do not have many competitors." (need 'do' for negative)
"How much distributors does he has?"
"How many distributors does he have?" (many + base form)
0 / 5
Oral Drilling

Say It in English

Describe what your company specializes in.
"We specialize in premium Brazilian gemstones and custom gold designs."
Explain that you do NOT sell directly to consumers.
"We do not sell directly to consumers. We work through distributors."
Describe your supply chain.
"We manage the entire supply chain — from sourcing to delivery."
Ask a potential partner about their experience with imports.
"Does your company have experience with imported goods?"
Talk about your competitors.
"We do not have many competitors in the premium segment."
Express interest in a partnership.
"I would like to discuss a possible partnership."
Sentence Building

Build the Sentence

inspecializesOur companygemstonespremium
"Our company specializes in premium gemstones."
notdirectlydoesHesellto consumers
"He does not sell directly to consumers."
workDoesclients?MiltonAmericanwith
"Does Milton work with American clients?"
supply chainmanagesthe entireOur company
"Our company manages the entire supply chain."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Present Your
Business

From guided to free production

Guided Role-play

Describe Your Products to a Buyer

Guided: Use the keywords

A potential buyer from California is visiting your office. Describe your products and how you sell them using the keywords below.
specializegemstoneswholesaledistributorssupply chainretailquality
Semi-free Role-play

Explain Your Operations

Semi-free: Complete the sentences

Sarah asks about your operations and supply chain. Complete each sentence:
"We specialize in ..."
"We do not sell ..."
"Our supply chain ..."
"Our competitors do not ..."
"Does your company ...?"
"I would like to discuss ..."
Free Role-play

Full Meeting with a Retail Partner

Free: No support — just talk!

You are meeting Sarah Chen, a luxury jewelry retailer from California, for the first time. Present your company, describe your products and operations, and discuss a possible partnership. No keywords, no script — just you.
Delayed Error Correction

Let's Fine-tune

Errors noted during free production

Write Milton's errors here during the live session.
Use the Zoom chat or whiteboard.
Chapter 7: Wrap-up

Wrapping
Up

Survival Card

5 Phrases to Survive

1
We specialize in luxury Brazilian gemstones.
2
Our supply chain covers sourcing to delivery.
3
We do not sell directly to consumers — we work through distributors.
4
Does your company have experience with imported goods?
5
I would like to discuss a possible partnership.
What I Learned

Lesson 2 Checklist

I can describe my company's products and specialization.
I know 10 business vocabulary words (retail, wholesale, etc.).
I can use Present Simple: affirmative, negative, and interrogative.
I had a meeting with a potential retail partner in English.
I can explain my supply chain without help.
I can ask business questions using "Does...?" correctly.
I know my 5 Survival Card phrases.
I corrected common Present Simple mistakes.
I completed a free meeting role-play without support.
I feel ready for Lesson 3.

Lesson 3 — Making Connections

Networking at
Trade Shows

B1+ — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review — Lesson 2

"What does your company specialize in?"
"Tell me about your supply chain."
"Do you work with distributors or retail partners?"
"How is your revenue this year?"
"Have you discussed any new partnerships?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Making Connections

"Have you ever attended a trade show in English?"
"What do you say when you meet a potential partner?"
"How do you introduce your company in 30 seconds?"
Key Expressions

Networking Collocations

make a good impression
to create a positive opinion about yourself
exchange business cards
to give and receive contact information
schedule a follow-up
to arrange a meeting after initial contact
Chapter 2: Packing Words

The Language of
Networking

10 essential words for trade shows

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

Building professional relationships
Networking
Building professional relationships
"We are actively networking at the show."
An exhibition for a specific industry
Trade Show
An exhibition for a specific industry
"I am attending a jewelry trade show."
A stand at a trade show
Booth
A stand at a trade show
"Your booth looks amazing."
A potential client or partner
Prospect
A potential client or partner
"Lisa is a strong prospect."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A short presentation to sell an idea
Pitch
A short presentation to sell an idea
"I like your pitch."
Contact after a meeting to continue
Follow-up
Contact after a meeting to continue
"Could I schedule a follow-up?"
A small card with contact information
Business Card
A small card with contact information
"Here is my business card."
To arrange a time for something
Schedule
To arrange a time for something
"Could I schedule a call?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 9-10

A particular type of business
Industry
A particular type of business
"The jewelry industry is competitive."
An opinion formed about someone
Impression
An opinion formed about someone
"Your collection made a great impression."
0 / 2 words revealed
Vocabulary Drill

Say Each Word

Networking Trade Show Booth Prospect Pitch Follow-up Business Card Schedule Industry Impression
Chapter 3: The Code

Present
Continuous

Current actions, temporary situations, future arrangements

Grammar Discovery

What do the orange words have in common?

"I am attending a trade show this week."
"We are showcasing our latest collection."
"She is looking for luxury jewelry suppliers."
"Milton is networking at the show."
The Rule

Present Continuous

AffirmativeSubject + am/is/are + verb-ing"I am attending a trade show."
NegativeSubject + am/is/are + not + verb-ing"He is not looking for wholesale partners."
InterrogativeAm/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?"Are you attending tomorrow?"
Common Mistake

Careful!

✗ "I am export jewelry every day."
✓ "I export jewelry every day." (routine = Simple)
✗ "He attends a trade show right now."
✓ "He is attending a trade show right now." (now = Continuous)
Grammar Practice

Fill in the Gaps

"Milton _____ a trade show."
"We _____ our latest collection."
"_____ you _____ the show tomorrow?"
"She _____ for luxury suppliers."
"We _____ our retail partnerships."

At the Trade Show

Milton meets Lisa Park

Dialogue — Part 1

At the Booth

Dialogue — Part 2

The Pitch

Dialogue — Part 3

The Follow-up

Dialogue Comprehension

Check Your Understanding

"What is Lisa looking for?"
"What is Milton currently doing at the show?"
"What did they schedule for next week?"

Practice

Find the Mistake

"I am attend a trade show this week."
"I am attending a trade show this week."
"She is look for luxury suppliers."
"She is looking for luxury suppliers."
"We are currently expand our partnerships."
"We are currently expanding our partnerships."
"Are you network at the show?"
"Are you networking at the show?"

Quick Fire

You are at a trade show. Tell someone what you are showcasing.
"I am showcasing our latest luxury jewelry collection."

Chapter 7: Wrap-up

Wrapping
Up

Survival phrases and review

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1 "I am attending a jewelry trade show this week."
2 "We are currently expanding our retail partnerships."
3 "Here is my business card. Could I schedule a follow-up?"
4 "Your collection made a great impression."
5 "I am looking forward to discussing a partnership."
Oral Drill

Repeat Each Phrase

"I am attending a jewelry trade show this week."
"We are showcasing our latest collection."
"She is looking for luxury suppliers."
"We are networking to find the right partners."
"I am looking forward to discussing a partnership."

Lesson 3 Checklist

I know 10 networking words.
I can use Present Continuous for current actions.
I can use Present Continuous for future arrangements.
I can pitch and network at trade shows in English.
I can schedule a follow-up.

Lesson 4 — Negotiation Essentials

Setting Terms and
Closing Deals

B1+ — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review — Lesson 3

"Tell me about the last trade show you attended."
"Did you meet any prospects?"
"How did you pitch your company?"
"Did you schedule any follow-ups?"
"What impression did you make at your booth?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Today's Topic

What do you usually negotiate?

"What terms do you discuss with distributors?"
"How do you handle price negotiations?"
"What is the most important thing in a deal?"
Chapter 2: Packing Words

The Language of
Negotiation

10 essential words

Vocabulary

Reveal the Words (1/2)

A formal suggestion or plan
Proposal
proposta
A response to an offer with different terms
Counteroffer
contraproposta
Conditions of an agreement
Terms
termos / condições
The last date by which something must be done
Deadline
prazo
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Reveal the Words (2/2)

Something given up in a negotiation
Concession
concessão
The difference between cost and selling price
Margin
margem
The quantity of goods traded
Volume
volume
An agreement where both sides give something up
Compromise
acordo / ceder
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Two More Words

Priority
Something that is more important than others
prioridade
Agreement
A decision reached by two or more parties
acordo / contrato
Chapter 3: The Code

The Art of
Polite English

Modal verbs for negotiation

Grammar Discovery

What Do These Have in Common?

"Could we discuss the terms?"
"I would suggest a 5% discount."
"We should review the deadline."
"Would you accept a larger volume?"

What pattern do you notice?

Grammar Rule

Modal Verbs for Negotiation

COULD — Polite Requests / Suggestions
"Could we discuss the terms?" / "Could you consider a lower price?"
WOULD — Hypothetical / Polite Offers
"Would you accept a larger volume?" / "I would suggest a 10% discount."
SHOULD — Advice / Recommendations
"We should review the deadline." / "You should consider our proposal."
Common Mistake

Can vs Could

✗ "I can offer 5%." (direct, less polite)
✓ "I could offer 5%." (polite, professional)
✗ "Can you lower the price?" (direct)
✓ "Could you consider a lower price?" (polite, professional)
Grammar Practice

Fill in the Modal

"_____ we discuss the terms?"
"I _____ suggest a 5% discount."
"We _____ review the deadline."
"_____ you accept a larger volume?"
"You _____ consider our proposal."
Chapter 4: Getting There

At the Negotiation Table

Milton meets David Chen

Dialogue — Part 1

The Proposal

Dialogue — Part 2

The Deadline

Dialogue — Part 3

The Agreement

Dialogue Comprehension

Check Your Understanding

"What discount could Milton offer?"
"What is a priority for David?"
"When could Milton have the terms ready?"
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Listening, exercises, and production

Listening

Listen First

0:000:00
Speed
"What is Milton negotiating with David?"
"What concession does Milton make?"
Listening

Listen Again

0:000:00
Speed
"What should they discuss before signing?"
"Would David prefer a shorter deadline?"
Business Document

Milton's Proposal

SAYEGH JEWELS
COMMERCIAL PROPOSAL
To: David Chen — Chen Distribution, TX
Product: Premium Brazilian Gemstone Collection
Volume: 500 units per quarter
Discount: 5% for orders above 500 units
Delivery: 45 days from order confirmation
Terms: 50% upfront, 50% on delivery
Valid until: June 30, 2026
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"Could you to offer a discount?"
"Could you offer a discount?" (no 'to' after modal)
"I would to suggest a longer deadline."
"I would suggest a longer deadline." (no 'to' after would)
"We should reviewing the terms."
"We should review the terms." (base form after should)
"Would you accepting a compromise?"
"Would you accept a compromise?" (base form after would)
0 / 4
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

A distributor asks for a discount. Make a polite suggestion.
"I could consider a 5% discount for that volume."
1 / 6
0 / 6
Quick Fire

Quick Fire — Round 2

You want to recommend reviewing the delivery deadline. What do you say?
"We should review the delivery deadline before signing."
1 / 6
0 / 6
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Your Turn

Role-play negotiation scenarios

Role-Play

Guided — Level 1

Price Negotiation
David asks for a discount. Offer terms and discuss the deadline.
I could offer... Would you accept... proposal terms
Role-Play

Semi-Free — Level 2

Full Negotiation
A distributor wants better terms. Negotiate the volume, margin, and deadline.
concession compromise margin deadline
Role-Play

Free — Level 3

Deal Closing
You are closing a deal with a major U.S. retailer. Negotiate and reach an agreement — no script!
proposal agreement volume priority
Chapter 7: Wrap-up

Wrapping
Up

Survival phrases and review

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1 "Could we discuss the terms of this agreement?"
2 "I would suggest a 5% discount for larger volumes."
3 "We should review the delivery deadline."
4 "Would you accept a compromise on the margin?"
5 "Could you send me the proposal by Friday?"
Oral Drill

Repeat Each Phrase

"Could we discuss the terms?"
"I would suggest a 5% discount."
"We should review the deadline."
"Would you accept a compromise?"
"I could make a concession on the timeline."
What I Learned

Lesson 4 Checklist

I know 10 negotiation words.
I can use Could for polite requests.
I can use Would for hypothetical offers.
I can use Should for advice.
I can negotiate terms and close deals in English.
Feedback

Teacher Feedback

Strengths
Note Milton's strong points here during the lesson.
Areas to Improve
Note areas for improvement during the lesson.
Homework

Your Mission

Your teacher will explain your homework now.

Stamp Earned!

Day 4: Negotiation Essentials — Complete

Summary

Key Vocabulary

Proposal
Counteroffer
Terms
Deadline
Concession
Margin
Volume
Compromise
Priority
Agreement
Recap

Key Takeaways

Could = polite requests and suggestions
Would = hypothetical and polite offers
Should = advice and recommendations
Modal + base form = never add 'to' or '-ing'

Lesson 5 — Board Meetings

Participating and
Expressing Opinions

B1+ — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review — Lesson 4

"Tell me about your last negotiation."
"Did you make a proposal?"
"What terms did you offer?"
"Did you reach a compromise?"
"Did both sides sign an agreement?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Today's Topic

Board meetings at your condo in Miami

"Have you ever had a difficult board meeting?"
"What topics do you usually discuss?"
"Have you ever made a motion at a meeting?"
Chapter 2: Packing Words

The Language of
Board Meetings

10 essential words

Vocabulary

Reveal the Words (1/2)

A list of topics to discuss at a meeting
Agenda
pauta / agenda
The written record of a meeting
Minutes
ata
A formal proposal at a meeting
Motion
moção
A financial plan for a period of time
Budget
orçamento
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Reveal the Words (2/2)

A person who rents a property
Tenant
inquilino / condômino
A formal decision made at a meeting
Resolution
resolução
An evaluation or judgment
Assessment
avaliação
Keeping a building or equipment in good condition
Maintenance
manutenção
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Two More Words

Approve
To officially accept or agree
aprovar
Adjourn
To end a meeting temporarily
encerrar (reunião)
Chapter 3: The Code

The Grammar of
Experience

Present Perfect: have/has + past participle

Grammar Discovery

What Do These Have in Common?

"I have attended many board meetings."
"Milton has exported jewelry for over thirty years."
"We have just approved the budget."
"Have you ever presented at a board meeting?"

What pattern do you notice?

Grammar Rule

Present Perfect for Experience

Experience (general, no specific time)
"I have attended many board meetings." / "Milton has exported jewelry for over thirty years."
Recent Events (just, already, yet)
"We have just approved the budget." / "The board has decided to increase fees."
Ever / Never Questions
"Have you ever presented at a board meeting?" / "I have never missed a meeting."
Common Mistake

Present Perfect vs Past Simple

✗ "I have attended the meeting yesterday."
✓ "I attended the meeting yesterday." (specific time = Past Simple)
✗ "I attended many meetings." (no specific time)
✓ "I have attended many meetings." (general experience = Present Perfect)
Grammar Practice

Fill in the Present Perfect

"I _____ the agenda."
"Milton _____ jewelry for over thirty years."
"_____ you ever presented at a meeting?"
"We _____ the budget."
"The tenants _____ concerns."
Chapter 4: Getting There

At the Board Meeting

Milton meets Karen White

Dialogue — Part 1

The Agenda

Dialogue — Part 2

The Budget

Dialogue — Part 3

The Resolution

Dialogue Comprehension

Check Your Understanding

"What has Milton prepared for the meeting?"
"How many complaints have they received?"
"Why have maintenance costs increased?"
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Listening, exercises, and production

Listening

Listen First

0:000:00
Speed
"What has the board reviewed?"
"What concerns have the tenants raised?"
Listening

Listen Again

0:000:00
Speed
"Has the board approved the budget?"
"Have the tenants received the minutes?"
Meeting Document

Board Meeting Agenda

OCEAN VIEW CONDOMINIUMS
BOARD MEETING AGENDA
Date: May 15, 2026 — 7:00 PM
President: Karen White
1. Call to order
2. Approval of previous minutes
3. Maintenance budget review
4. Assessment fee discussion
5. New safety regulations
6. Motions and resolutions
7. Adjournment
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"I have attended the meeting yesterday."
"I attended the meeting yesterday." (specific time = Past Simple)
"Milton have exported jewelry for 30 years."
"Milton has exported jewelry for 30 years." (he/she/it = has)
"Has you ever presented at a meeting?"
"Have you ever presented at a meeting?" (you = have)
"We have just approve the budget."
"We have just approved the budget." (have + past participle)
0 / 4
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

The board president asks if you have reviewed the agenda. What do you say?
"Yes, I have. I have also prepared some notes on the maintenance budget."
1 / 6
0 / 6
Quick Fire

Quick Fire — Round 2

Several tenants have complained about the fees. How do you report this?
"Several tenants have expressed their concerns. We have received five complaints this month."
1 / 6
0 / 6
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Your Turn

Role-play board meeting scenarios

Role-Play

Guided — Level 1

Budget Discussion
The board president asks about the budget. Report what you have reviewed and what the tenants have said.
I have reviewed... Have you received... agenda budget
Role-Play

Semi-Free — Level 2

Full Board Meeting
Participate in a full meeting. Discuss the agenda, make a motion, and vote on a resolution.
motion resolution assessment maintenance
Role-Play

Free — Level 3

Difficult Meeting
A tenant disagrees with the budget increase. Lead the discussion, address concerns, and reach a resolution — no script!
approve adjourn tenant budget
Chapter 7: Wrap-up

Wrapping
Up

Survival phrases and review

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1 "I have reviewed the agenda and prepared some notes."
2 "I would like to make a motion regarding the budget."
3 "Have you received the minutes from the last meeting?"
4 "Several tenants have expressed concerns about the fees."
5 "I move to approve the resolution."
Oral Drill

Repeat Each Phrase

"I have reviewed the agenda."
"Have you received the minutes?"
"The tenants have expressed concerns."
"I move to approve the resolution."
"We should adjourn the meeting."
What I Learned

Lesson 5 Checklist

I know 10 board meeting words.
I can use Present Perfect for experience.
I can use Present Perfect for recent events.
I can use Ever/Never questions correctly.
